We establish a rigorous baseline using a 12-layer BanglaBERT model combined with a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer for exact-boundary entity detection. To address deployment constraints, we compress this teacher model into a 4-layer student network through Knowledge Distillation (KD), where the student learns from the teacher's pre-CRF soft emission logits. Finally, we apply INT8 dynamic quantization to further reduce model size and inference cost. Our final quantized student achieves an 8.6x CPU speedup while requiring nearly 48 percent less storage than the CRF teacher model.

</details>


### [288] [TypedCSIP: Typed Counterfactual Pretraining for Chinese Legislative Conflict Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25474)
*Yao Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TypedCSIP introduces a typed counterfactual pretraining method for the LCR-CN conflict-classification benchmark, showing improved performance over baselines. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to improve conflict classification in legal texts by leveraging expert-written counterfactual data and to address shortcomings in existing methods for handling nuanced legal doctrine classifications.

Method: The method involves a two-stage training process. Stage 1 pretrains a shared encoder using a typed Counterfactual Selective Intervention Pretraining objective, utilizing expert-revised counterfactuals. Stage 2 fine-tunes this encoder to perform five-way conflict classification.

Result: The approach achieves significant performance gains in macro-F1 scores on the test dataset, with the gains verified using extensive statistical testing across different models. It also provides positive results on unseen records, though the approach does not generalize to other legal tasks like superior-law retrieval.

Conclusion: TypedCSIP is effective for conflict classification tasks in legal texts, demonstrating its specialization and providing reproducible resources for further research, but its scope is limited to classification.

Abstract: TypedCSIP is a typed counterfactual pretraining method for the conflict-classification task of the LCR-CN benchmark (Zhao et al.,
  2026): given a (superior, subordinate) provision pair, predict whether the pair conflicts and which of four legal-doctrine types
  (Responsibility, Condition, Sanction, Definition) describes the inconsistency. We exploit LCR-CN's expert-written minimal revisions
  as training-time counterfactual supervision; at test time the classifier reads only the original pair.
  Stage 1 pretrains a shared encoder with a typed Counterfactual Selective Intervention Pretraining objective on (superior,
  subordinate, expert-revised) triplets, treating the expert revision as a counterfactual that the typed factor head must classify as
  carrying no conflict evidence. Stage 2 transfers the encoder to a five-way classification head.
  The confirmatory test was registered on the Open Science Framework before observing v6 measurements: 18 seeds, locked rule requiring
  mean per-seed difference at least 0.8 pp with both seed-bootstrap and Student-t 95% lower bounds above zero. On the 696-record test
  split, the v2 variant improves macro-F1 over the strongest single-model baseline by +0.916 pp on chinese-roberta-wwm-ext and +1.288
  pp on the SAILER cross-backbone replication; both cells pass the rule. A cold-start stratified result on the 244 Unseen-gB records
  keeps the gain positive on both backbones.
  A cross-task diagnostic shows the Stage-2 encoder is classification-specialized and does not transfer to LCR-CN's superior-law
  retrieval task, so we scope the contribution to conflict classification. We release code, 72 pre-registered prediction files,
  matched-seed and MLM-control auxiliaries, and the OSF pre-registration record.

</details>


### [289] [IndexMem: Learned KV-Cache Eviction with Latent Memory for Long-Context LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25475)
*Xintong Yang,Hao Gu,Binxing Xu,Lujun Li,Bei Liu,Jiacheng Liu,Qiyuan Zhu,Sirui Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to enhance long-context inference for large language models (LLMs) by using a learnable indexer and a latent memory module to improve token retention and retrieval under a bounded KV budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current softmax attention mechanisms in LLMs struggle with long contexts due to the KV cache growing linearly with sequence length, leading to inefficiency. Traditional token eviction policies are heuristic and fail to capture token importance effectively.

Method: The authors propose a learnable indexer to predict key-value (KV) importance for better token retention and a lightweight latent memory module that compresses and updates evicted tokens into a state for residual readouts, mitigating irreversible forgetting.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates consistent improvements on multiple benchmarks such as RULER (4K/16K), Needle-in-a-Haystack retrieval, and LongBench scores across various LLMs like Qwen, Mistral, and Llama, even under aggressive KV eviction.

Conclusion: This approach offers a robust solution for long-context inference in LLMs by balancing KV budget constraints with improved token importance prediction and memory recall, outperforming existing eviction strategies.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly expected to operate over long contexts, yet standard softmax attention incurs a KV cache that grows linearly with sequence length, quickly becoming the bottleneck for long context inference. A practical remedy is to evict less important KV entries; however, existing eviction policies are largely heuristic and struggle to capture the rich, input-dependent distribution of token importance. In this work, we introduce a learnable indexer that predicts KV importance, enabling more accurate retention of critical tokens. Meanwhile, naively evicting tokens permanently discards their information, leading to irreversible forgetting and degraded retrieval over long ranges. To address this, we propose a lightweight latent memory module that compresses evicted tokens into a compact, online-updated state and provides residual readouts to compensate for the attention contributions lost through KV eviction. Collectively, our method enables accurate long-context inference under a bounded KV budget, delivering consistent improvements on RULER (4K/16K) across Qwen, Mistral, and Llama models (up to 25 points under aggressive eviction), markedly more stable Needle-in-a-Haystack retrieval, and superior LongBench scores and compression curves compared to existing eviction policies.

</details>


### [290] [Retrieval as Reasoning: Self-Evolving Agent-Native Retrieval via LLM-Wiki](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25480)
*Haoliang Ming,Feifei Li,Xiaoqing Wu,Wenhui Que*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LLM-Wiki, a novel retrieval system for LLMs that uses a Retrieval-as-Reasoning paradigm, outperforming baselines on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve upon traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems by enabling LLMs to perform reasoning-oriented retrieval rather than simple one-shot context fetching.

Method: The authors propose LLM-Wiki, which organizes external knowledge into structured Wiki pages with bidirectional links, supports reasoning through tool-calling interfaces, and incorporates an Error Book for self-correction.

Result: LLM-Wiki outperforms seven baselines, achieving up to 8.1 F1 points improvement, and demonstrates superior performance on multi-document structured queries.

Conclusion: LLM-Wiki effectively operationalizes Retrieval-as-Reasoning, proving that structured, composable knowledge can enhance LLM agents' capabilities across various benchmarks and reasoning tasks.

Abstract: LLM agents require retrieval to behave less like one-shot context fetching and more like reasoning: searching, reading, traversing, and deciding when evidence is sufficient. However, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) typically organizes external knowledge as flat chunks retrieved by embedding similarity, exposing a retrieval-as-lookup interface that is poorly aligned with tool-using agents. We propose LLM-Wiki, an agent-native retrieval system that operationalizes the Retrieval-as-Reasoning paradigm by treating external knowledge as a compilable, composable, and self-evolving structure rather than a static retrieval index. LLM-Wiki compiles documents into structured Wiki pages with bidirectional links, exposes search, read, and link-following operations through standard tool-calling interfaces, and introduces an Error Book for persistent structural and semantic self-correction. On HotpotQA, MuSiQue, and 2WikiMultiHopQA, LLM-Wiki outperforms seven baselines, including HippoRAG 2, LightRAG, and GraphRAG, with gains of 2.0-8.1 F1 points over the strongest graph-based baseline and larger gains over Dense RAG. On AuthTrace, LLM-Wiki achieves the best overall accuracy, with especially strong gains on multi-document structured queries, showing that compilation-based knowledge organization generalizes beyond chain-style multi-hop reasoning.

</details>


### [291] [A Controlled Synthetic Benchmark for Educational Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25502)
*Yehudit Aperstein,Alexander Apartsin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a synthetic benchmark dataset for educational sentiment analysis (ABSA), addressing the lack of aspect-labeled student feedback, and evaluates various baseline models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a scarcity of aspect-labeled student feedback in educational reviews due to their private, institution-specific, and expensive-to-annotate nature. The study seeks to address this gap by creating a synthetic benchmark.

Method: The authors generated a synthetic dataset of 10,000 course reviews using a 20-aspect schema and a realism-tuned prompt refined through a multi-cycle judge-editor process. Various baseline models, including BERT and GPT, were evaluated for performance.

Result: BERT's performance reached a micro-F1 of 0.2930 with tuning, while GPT-5.2 achieved a zero-shot micro-F1 of 0.2519. External evaluations showed partial transfer from synthetic to real data, achieving a micro-F1 of 0.4593 on real-world feedback.

Conclusion: The study successfully creates and validates a synthetic benchmark for educational ABSA, providing a reproducible tool for a field where labeled datasets are scarce.

Abstract: Educational aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) can support course improvement, but public aspect-labeled student feedback remains scarce because educational reviews are private, institution-specific, and expensive to annotate. This study introduces a controlled synthetic benchmark for educational ABSA built from 10,000 synthetic course reviews with explicit train-validation-test splits and a 20-aspect pedagogical schema spanning instructional quality, assessment and course management, learning demand, learning environment, and engagement. The corpus is generated with sampled target labels, sampled nuance attributes, and a realism-tuned prompt refined through a three-cycle judge-editor procedure. On the resulting benchmark, local baselines with TF-IDF, two-step transformers, and joint encoders show that the task is nontrivial; the strongest untuned model, BERT, reaches a held-out detection micro-F1 of 0.2760, while a modest lower-rate BERT schedule improves this to 0.2930. Full-test GPT-based inference with gpt-5.2 reaches 0.2519 micro-F1 in zero-shot mode and 0.2501 with retrieval-based few-shot prompting, placing batch inference above the classical baseline and close to the compact joint encoders. A conservative external evaluation on 2,829 mapped student-feedback reviews from Herath et al. yields a micro-F1 of 0.4593 for BERT on a 9-aspect overlap, indicating partial synthetic-to-real transfer. Realism and faithfulness analyses are reported as generator diagnostics that clarify how the benchmark was stabilized and where label noise remains. The study therefore contributes a synthetic educational ABSA corpus, a documented generation procedure, and a reproducible benchmark setting for a domain in which public labeled data remain difficult to obtain.

</details>


### [292] [The Age of Curiosity Meets the Age of AI: Benchmarking Child Safety in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25510)
*Samee Arif,Angana Borah,Rada Mihalcea*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Children now have increased exposure to Large Language Models (LLMs), raising safety concerns due to the potential for developmentally unsuitable responses. To address this, researchers introduced KIDBench, a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate child-facing safety in LLMs based on developmental psychology. KIDBench includes realistic queries and simulations for children aged 7–11, with methods like implicit- and explicit-cue prompting improving responses. The study also provides tools like KIDGuardLlama and KIDLlama for safer child-facing AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for this research is the increasing exposure of children to LLMs, which may deliver responses that are developmentally inappropriate or lack guidance suitable for children. Current evaluations of LLM safety do not specifically address child-facing concerns, necessitating a dedicated benchmark to fill this gap.

Method: The researchers created KIDBench, a developmental-psychology-grounded benchmark, consisting of realistic child-oriented queries and simulations. They tested the safety of LLM responses using no cues, implicit cues (suggesting child context), and explicit age-based guidance. Additionally, they conducted cross-lingual and cultural evaluations and multi-turn simulations.

Result: The study found that using implicit cues improves safety scores by 9–47%, while explicit age-based guidance adds a further 10–30% gain across LLMs. However, safety behaviors varied significantly across languages and cultural contexts. Multi-turn simulations revealed a 6–24% degradation in response quality over time. The researchers also introduced KIDGuardLlama and KIDLlama to help generate safer content.

Conclusion: This study highlights the need for child-specific evaluations of LLMs, demonstrating effectiveness with tools like KIDBench, KIDGuardLlama, and KIDLlama. The research underscores the importance of considering implicit and explicit context-based cues to improve safety, but also reveals room for improvement in cross-lingual and multi-turn scenarios.

Abstract: Children increasingly have access to Large Language Models (LLMs), which may expose them to responses that are developmentally inappropriate or require age-sensitive safety, guidance, and boundaries. Existing LLM safety evaluations largely focus on harmful-content avoidance and do not explicitly target child-facing safety. We introduce KIDBench, a benchmark for evaluating child-facing LLM safety for ages 7--11 using a developmental-psychology-grounded LLM-as-a-Judge rubric. KIDBench contains realistic child queries across ten categories, with single-turn prompts and multi-turn child-actor simulations. We compare no-cues prompts with no child context, implicit-cues prompts that suggest a child speaker, and explicit age instructions. Implicit-cues improve scores by 9--47% across models, while explicit age adds a further 10--30% gain. Cross-lingual and cultural evaluations show uneven safety behavior across languages and country contexts. Multi-turn simulations show that child-facing response quality can degrade by 6--24% from the first to worst turn. Beyond evaluation, we introduce KIDGuardLlama, a child-safety evaluator, and KIDLlama, a child-oriented response model, showing how KIDBench supports safer child-facing AI

</details>


### [293] [CRPO: Character-centric Group Relative Policy Optimization for Role-aware Reasoning in Role-playing Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25511)
*Yihong Tang,Kehai Chen,Liang Yue,Benyou Wang,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study identifies the limitations of current RL methods like GRPO in role-playing tasks, introducing a new framework, CRPO, to enhance character fidelity and style through three distinct approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the weaknesses of GRPO and similar RL methods in maintaining character alignment during role-playing tasks, where optimization methods often result in style collapse and reduced character fidelity.

Method: The authors propose CRPO, which enhances character distinctiveness through three methods: 1) Separating task logic from stylistic rewards, 2) Dynamically adjusting constraints based on character complexity, and 3) Using generic responses as baselines to counteract generic outputs.

Result: CRPO shows superior performance compared to existing methods in key attributes such as consistency and emotion in role-playing scenarios, demonstrated through extensive experimental validation.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that CRPO effectively addresses the challenge of aligning RL objectives with role-playing tasks, outperforming prior techniques in maintaining character distinctiveness and style.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Reinforcement Learning (RL), particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), have significantly enhanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models. However, applying these problem-centric optimization methods to role-playing agents often leads to a loss of character fidelity and style collapse, as they prioritize context-specific utility over persona alignment. To address this, we propose Character-Centric Group Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), a framework designed to realign RL objectives with the role-playing task. CRPO improves character distinctiveness through three mechanisms: decoupling task logic from stylistic rewards to resolve gradient conflicts, dynamically adapting optimization constraints based on character complexity, and utilizing generic responses as negative baselines to prevent the model from reverting to a common distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CRPO outperforms existing methods in consistency, emotion and others.

</details>


### [294] [Is Inference Mediated by Distinct Semantic Structures in LLMs? A Mechanistic Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25520)
*Nura Aljaafari,Marco Valentino,André Freitas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study investigates if Transformer models encode semantic operations, not just label predictions, using an analysis in Natural Language Inference contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether Transformer models represent the semantic transformations producing labels or simply predict the labels without capturing the underlying operations.

Method: The researchers use controlled premise-hypothesis pairs differing by semantic transformation. They employ SVD on layer-wise activations to estimate operation-level subspaces and test their importance through activation steering in decoder models.

Result: Transformation effects are decodable with up to 99% accuracy and occupy overlapping subspaces that influence predictions causally, but steerability and independence vary across models.

Conclusion: Transformer models partially encode how a hypothesis relates to a premise, suggesting that analysis and control should focus on semantic operations rather than just predicted labels.

Abstract: Predicting a label correctly does not necessarily require representing the operation that produces it. Transformer representations are known to carry label-level information, but whether they encode semantic operations producing those labels is unclear. We investigate this in Natural Language Inference using controlled premise-hypothesis pairs that differ by a single semantic transformation. Using layer-wise activations, we estimate operation-level subspaces via SVD and test their causal relevance through activation steering in four open-weight decoder models. Transformation effects are decodable with $84.8$-$99\%$ accuracy and occupy partially distinct but overlapping subspaces, exceeding random-subspace baselines. Steering experiments show that these directions causally influence predictions, though steerability varies across models; cross-operation steering further reveals structured interference and a dissociation between subspace selectivity and cross-operation independence. These findings indicate that the models encode not only that a hypothesis relates to a premise but also, in part, how it does so, implying that mechanistic analysis and control should operate at the level of semantic operations rather than predicted labels alone.

</details>


### [295] [BC Protocol: Structured Dual-Expert Dialogue for Eliciting High-Quality Chain-of-Thought Post-Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25549)
*Bo Zou,Chao Xu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the BC Protocol, a dual-expert method to produce high-quality chain-of-thought (CoT) data for post-training large language models (LLMs), demonstrating superior reasoning quality compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a bottleneck in creating high-quality CoT data for LLMs, as existing approaches like crowdsourcing, expert solo writing, and RLHF have significant limitations in terms of reasoning chain depth and quality.

Method: The BC Protocol involves pairing a domain expert (crystallized intelligence) with a knowledge engineer (fluid intelligence) to externalize implicit expert judgments into detailed reasoning chains. The framework also introduces participant aptitude modeling and emphasizes personnel selection over process design.

Result: A controlled experiment in narrative fiction showed the BC Protocol significantly outperformed solo expert writing in reasoning 'naturalness,' with statistical validation from three judge models.

Conclusion: The dual-expert BC Protocol is an effective approach for eliciting high-quality CoT data, addressing limitations in current methods, and providing a promising direction for enhancing LLM post-training.

Abstract: High-quality expert chain-of-thought (CoT) data is one of the core bottlenecks in large language model (LLM) post-training. Existing data production methods each have structural limitations: crowdsourced annotation lacks deep reasoning paths; expert solo writing is constrained by the "expert blind spot" -- experts structurally skip reasoning steps they consider obvious; RLHF only produces preference signals rather than reasoning chains.
  This paper proposes the BC Protocol -- a structured dual-expert elicitation method for LLM post-training data production. The method carefully pairs a domain expert (crystallized intelligence) with a knowledge engineer (fluid intelligence), systematically externalizing the expert's implicit judgments as natural language reasoning chains. We introduce the Participant Aptitude Model, which defines six participant characteristic dimensions that affect elicitation quality. "Calibrated Ignorance" is an original concept proposed in this paper. We further propose "Selection-over-Prescription" as a methodological principle: for implicit knowledge elicitation tasks, investing quality-control resources in personnel selection yields a higher return than investing the same resources in process design.
  In a controlled experiment in the narrative fiction domain, we directly compared CoT produced by BC Protocol dual dialogue (Group A, (n=20)) against CoT written independently by the same domain expert (Group B, (n=20)). Three cross-vendor judge models -- GPT-4o, Claude Opus 4.5, and Gemini 2.5 Pro -- conducted blind evaluation across five dimensions (600 ratings total). Results show that the BC Protocol achieves an overwhelming advantage in "naturalness of reasoning process" (Group A mean 4.80 vs. Group B mean 1.30, (p=2.4\times10^{-8}), Cliff's (δ=1.0)).

</details>


### [296] [PennySynth: RAG-Driven Data Synthesis for Automated Quantum Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25572)
*Minghao Shao,Nouhaila Innan,Hariharan Janardhanan,Muhammad Kashif,Alberto Marchisio,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PennySynth is a retrieval-augmented framework addressing the shortcomings of general-purpose LLMs in quantum programming by integrating a code-aware approach with a curated knowledge base.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the inaccuracies and limitations of general-purpose LLMs when handling specialized quantum programming tasks in PennyLane, such as hallucinating gate names or misplacing device configurations.

Method: A retrieval-augmented generation framework, PennySynth, was developed with a curated knowledge base of 13,389 instruction-code pairs. It uses a code-aware embedding strategy via st-codesearch-distilroberta-base for efficient natural-language-to-code retrieval.

Result: PennySynth significantly outperformed baseline models, achieving 64%, 68%, and 52% pass@5 scores on QHack challenges for 2022, 2023, and 2024, respectively, with substantial improvements over Claude Sonnet 4.6.

Conclusion: Code-aware embeddings and curated datasets play pivotal roles in advancing LLM capabilities in specialized quantum programming, highlighting the distinct nature of structural and functional correctness in evaluating quantum code quality.

Abstract: The growing complexity of quantum programming frameworks has exposed a critical limitation in existing large language model (LLM)-based code assistants: general-purpose models hallucinate PennyLane-specific gate names, misplace device configurations, and produce structurally invalid circuits when faced with specialized quantum coding challenges. We present PennySynth, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that addresses this gap by conditioning LLM inference on a curated knowledge base of 13,389 PennyLane instruction-code pairs, built via a three-stage extraction, verification, and deduplication pipeline over official PennyLane repositories, community GitHub sources, and QHack competition archives. PennySynth introduces a code-aware embedding strategy using st-codesearch-distilroberta-base, trained for natural-language-to-code retrieval, increasing average retrieval cosine similarity from 0.45 to 0.726 compared to a general-purpose baseline. Evaluated across 74 challenges spanning three years of the QHack competition (2022, 2023, 2024), PennySynth achieves 64%, 68%, and 52% pass@5 on QHack 2022, 2023, and 2024, respectively, improving over Claude Sonnet 4.6 without retrieval by +28, +25, and +28 percentage points. We further introduce a quantum-adapted CodeBLEU metric that upweights qml.* token patterns and show that structural code similarity and functional correctness capture distinct aspects of quantum code quality. Controlled ablations reveal that code-aware embeddings are the primary driver of retrieval performance, while dataset expansion and source composition provide additional gains when retrieval quality is sufficiently precise.

</details>


### [297] [Multilingual Phonological Feature Recognition with Self-Supervised Speech Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25596)
*Abner Hernandez,Tomás Arias-Vergara,Daiqi Liu,Andreas Maier,Paula Andrea Pérez-Toro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PhonoQ-2.0 is a multilingual system for recognizing phonological features directly, achieving strong performance across languages in both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a universal, linguistically-grounded way to represent speech through direct estimation of phonological features instead of relying on phoneme outputs.

Method: PhonoQ-2.0 predicts 22-dimensional phonological feature vectors per frame using a manner-conditioned gating mechanism on self-supervised speech models, ensuring structured and accurate predictions.

Result: PhonoQ-2.0 achieves high macro-F1 scores: 91.3% in-domain, 88.9% out-of-domain, and shows significant improvement in unseen language evaluations compared to phoneme-based baselines.

Conclusion: PhonoQ-2.0 improves the prediction of phonological features across languages and domains significantly, highlighting the potential of using linguistically-grounded methods over traditional phoneme-based approaches.

Abstract: Phonological features provide a language-general and linguistically grounded representation of speech. We present PhonoQ-2.0, a multilingual frame-level phonological feature recognizer built on self-supervised speech models. The system directly predicts a structured 22-dimensional feature vector per frame encoding manner, vowel quality, place, and voicing, instead of deriving features from phoneme outputs. To ensure phonologically coherent predictions, we introduce a manner-conditioned gating mechanism that activates valid feature groups. Evaluated across multiple languages and corpora, PhonoQ-2.0 achieves an average macro-F1 of 91.3% in-domain and 88.9% out-of-domain. Compared to a strong CTC phoneme baseline, it delivers consistent gains of +8.8 F1 in-domain and +8.6 out-of-domain on average. In unseen-language evaluation, PhonoQ-2.0 improves macro-F1 from 66.9% to 73.6% (+6.7 on average), with gains of up to +10.8 points.

</details>


### [298] [Toward a Benchmark for Controllable Simulation of Imperfect Students with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25601)
*Alexander Apartsin,Omri Sason,Yehudit Aperstein*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores the use of large language models (LLMs) to simulate students with specific skill profiles, enabling targeted teacher training. It introduces a framework to selectively control retained and suppressed competencies in AI-simulated learners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to provide tools for teacher education by leveraging AI to simulate learners with targeted skill profiles, allowing educators to practice diagnosing and responding to varied student needs.

Method: The researchers developed a framework with skill vectors to represent simulated students and used prompt-based controls to modify skill retention and suppression. They evaluated the models using metrics like profile alignment and comparisons of retained versus forgotten skills.

Result: The study found that partial mastery simulation is possible and measurable within a mathematics context, although controllability outcomes vary significantly depending on the language model used.

Conclusion: Controllable learner simulation introduces an important research domain that supports teacher education by intersecting educational simulation and advancements in language-model control.

Abstract: Teacher education requires deliberate practice with learners who exhibit identifiable strengths, weaknesses, and partial mastery. Large language models could support such practice by simulating students with known skill components, enabling teachers to rehearse explanations, diagnoses, and instructional responses. For this purpose, however, the central requirement is neither to maximize benchmark accuracy nor to suppress isolated facts, but to control model behavior so that it reflects a specified skill profile. This paper investigates whether prompted language models can be steered to retain some skills while suppressing others. We introduce a benchmark-oriented framework in which an explicit skill vector represents a simulated student, prompt-based control specifies retained and missing competencies, and behavior is evaluated using profile-alignment metrics, retained-versus-forgotten comparisons, and cross-skill calibration analyses. The results show that selective partial mastery can be induced and measured in a structured mathematics setting, although the degree of controllability remains model-dependent. These findings position controllable learner simulation as a distinct research problem at the intersection of teacher education, educational simulation, and language-model control.

</details>


### [299] [DVAO: Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization for Multi-reward Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25604)
*Guochao Jiang,Jingyi Song,Guofeng Quan,Chuzhan Hao,Guohua Liu,Yuewei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization (DVAO), which improves reinforcement learning methods for aligning large language models by dynamically adjusting combination weights in multi-reward settings, leading to more stable training and better results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from inefficiencies and instability in existing methods like Reward Combination and Advantage Combination when applied to multi-reward scenarios in reinforcement learning for large language models.

Method: The authors introduce DVAO, which dynamically adjusts combination weights based on the empirical reward variance for each objective, emphasizing stronger learning signals and diminishing noisy ones. They also prove mathematically that this approach stabilizes advantage magnitudes and incorporates cross-objective regularization.

Result: The experiments using Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 models on mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks show that DVAO achieves superior results, including a better Pareto frontier and more robust training stability than baseline methods.

Conclusion: Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization addresses the key limitations of previous scalarization practices and improves training outcomes, offering a significant improvement in efficiency and stability for reinforcement learning in multi-reward settings.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning has become a standard paradigm for aligning Large Language Models with human intent and task requirements. While Group Relative Policy Optimization offers an efficient, value-model-free alternative to Proximal Policy Optimization, adapting it to real-world multi-reward settings remains challenging. Standard scalarization practices, such as Reward Combination and Advantage Combination, suffer from significant drawbacks: Reward Combination frequently generates advantages with excessively large squared magnitudes that lead to training instability, while Advantage Combination relies on static hyperparameters and ignores cross-objective correlations. To address these limitations, we propose Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization (DVAO), which dynamically adjusts combination weights based on the empirical reward variance of each objective within a rollout group, effectively up-weighting objectives with a stronger learning signal while suppressing noisy ones. We mathematically prove that DVAO maintains bounded advantage magnitudes for stable training and introduces a self-adaptive cross-objective regularization mechanism. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks using Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 models demonstrate that DVAO significantly outperforms baseline methods, achieving a superior multi-objective Pareto frontier and robust training stability.

</details>


### [300] [Beyond Literal Translation: Evaluating Cultural Effectiveness in Social Media UGC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25626)
*Linjuan Wu,Ruiqi Zhang,Xinze Lyu,Ye Guo,Daoxin Zhang,Zhe Xu,Yao Hu,Yixin Cao,Yongliang Shen,Weiming Lu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CULTURE-MT, a benchmark for translating social media content that accounts for cultural and emotional nuances while providing an evaluation platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translation of user-generated content on social media remains challenging due to informal style, cultural references, and the lack of suitable benchmarks capturing cultural and emotional nuance.

Method: The authors create the CULTURE-MT benchmark using 1,002 UGC notes across 14 domains, categorizing data into four cultural styles, fine-tune baseline models, and propose a new evaluation criterion called cultural effectiveness.

Result: The authors observe that traditional metrics do not effectively measure cultural effectiveness and find that translation quality improves with model size in base LLMs.

Conclusion: CULTURE-MT and the introduced cultural effectiveness metric provide a robust framework to better evaluate UGC translation models, offering an open platform to foster advancements in this area.

Abstract: Social media platforms enable large-scale cross-lingual communication, but translating user-generated content (UGC) remains challenging due to its informal style, cultural references, and interaction-based expressions. While recent LLMs have improved translation quality, existing benchmarks and metrics often fail to capture whether translations convey intended meaning and cultural resonance in real-world settings. In this work, we introduce CULTURE-MT, a benchmark for social media translation that focuses on both CULtural Transmission and UGC-specific emotion REsonance. CULTURE-MT consists of 1,002 UGC notes across 14 domains, categorized into four types based on culture-loaded symbols and linguistic style features. We also construct UGC-oriented training data to fine-tune Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B as baselines. We propose cultural effectiveness as a new evaluation criterion, focusing on expression accuracy and cultural adaptability. Testing 15 models, including the baselines, we find that traditional metrics fail to capture cultural effectiveness. We also observe that cultural effectiveness on base LLMs correlates with model size. Our work provides a comprehensive evaluation system for UGC translation models and will offer an open evaluation platform to advance research in this area. We release the CULTURE-MT benchmark and provide an online leaderboard where submitted translation results can be evaluated by our trained JUDGER.

</details>


### [301] [When In-Distribution Gains Fail: Evaluating Weak-to-Strong Reward Models under Preference Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25629)
*Khoi Le,Tri Cao,Phong Nguyen,Cong-Duy Nguyen,Anh Tuan Luu,Miao Chunyan,See-Kiong Ng,Thong Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Weak-to-strong (W2S) generalization can fail in zero-shot distribution shifts as students trained on weak preferences appear successful in-distribution but fail in new contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of scalable oversight in W2S preference learning, especially under zero-shot distribution shifts.

Method: The authors propose Representation Anchoring (Anchor), a regularizer that limits shifts from pretrained representations during fine-tuning while allowing task-specific adaptation.

Result: Anchor improves out-of-distribution preference transfer performance without sacrificing in-distribution accuracy across various domains, datasets, and model families.

Conclusion: The study highlights representational failure in W2S and provides methods to improve robustness, paving the way for better preference transfer.

Abstract: Weak-to-strong (W2S) generalization is a promising framework for scalable oversight, yet existing evaluations often test students under matched train--test distributions. Therefore, we study W2S preference learning under zero-shot distribution shift and find that strong students trained on weak preference labels can appear successful in-distribution while failing to transfer across preference datasets. We provide evidence for a representational failure mode in which weak-supervised fine-tuning can pull the strong model toward source-domain features instead of maintaining broadly transferable preference representations. To mitigate this, we propose Representation Anchoring (Anchor), a simple yet effective regularizer that constrains excessive drift from the pretrained strong model's representation space during fine-tuning, while still allowing task-relevant adaptation. Across preference domains, datasets, and model families, Anchor consistently improves out-of-distribution transfer while maintaining competitive in-distribution performance. Together, our evaluation protocol, transfer-aware metrics, and method expose hidden brittleness in current W2S reward modeling and provide a practical path toward more robust preference transfer.

</details>


### [302] [Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25638)
*Qi He,Huan Chen,Ya Guo,Huijia Zhu,Yi R. Fung,Baojian Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel training method called Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback (RLDF) to improve policy loss estimation for diffusion language models (dLLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Policy loss estimation remains a significant challenge in reinforcement learning for dLLMs, necessitating a more effective and computationally efficient approach.

Method: The paper proposes RLDF, which optimizes the model by targeting the clipped clean state from noisy intermediate states and employs weighted timestep sampling.

Result: RLDF shows consistent and significant performance and generalizability improvements across two dLLM architectures (LLaDA and Dream) on multiple reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: RLDF provides a scalable and principled foundation for reinforcement learning in diffusion language models, supported by the introduction of the Drift framework.

Abstract: Policy loss estimation remains a fundamental and long-standing challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) for diffusion language models (dLLMs). We introduce Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback (RLDF), a novel training paradigm that leverages feedback obtained from rollout and training processes to facilitate accurate and efficient policy loss estimation. To balance the trade-off between computational efficiency and estimation effectiveness, RLDF optimizes the model toward the clipped clean state $\hat{x}_0$ from intermediate noisy states $x_t$, combined with weighted timestep sampling over $t$. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RLDF achieves consistent and substantial improvements in both performance and generalizability across two representative dLLM architectures, LLaDA and Dream, on multiple reasoning benchmarks. Our work lays a principled foundation for scalable reinforcement learning in diffusion language models. We build Drift, a training framework for dLLMs, available at https://github.com/ant-research/Drift.

</details>


### [303] [Iterate Until Retrieved: Factual Nugget Optimization for Discoverable Continual Corrections in Agentic RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25641)
*Moshe Hazoom,Gal Patel,Alon Talmor,Tom Hope*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a method, Iterative Nugget Optimization (INO), to process and optimize actionable factual feedback in B2B agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, improving information retrieval and usability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the processing of actionable factual corrections from feedback in complex B2B agentic RAG systems, aiming to transform them into effective and discoverable knowledge-base entries.

Method: The authors propose INO, an index-time optimization framework that iteratively generates, tests, and improves factual knowledge pieces (nuggets) by simulating queries and analyzing failed attempts to improve their discoverability.

Result: The application of INO in two B2B knowledge agents—a product support agent and a support ticket agent—demonstrated consistent improvement in discoverability and effectiveness of factual corrections, confirmed through automated and human evaluations.

Conclusion: INO provides a systematic and effective way to handle and optimize factual feedback, enhancing RAG systems' ability to deliver accurate and usable knowledge in complex B2B environments.

Abstract: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems in complex B2B (business-to-business) settings may often receive free-form response feedback. Rather than generic feedback signals such as style, preference, or overall response quality, we focus on actionable factual corrections. We identify these instances and convert them into compact knowledge-base entries, which we call factual nuggets. We introduce Iterative Nugget Optimization (INO), an index-time optimization method that uses the production agentic RAG as a test harness: it creates an initial nugget, probes it with the triggering query and paraphrases, reflects over failed retrieval and answer traces, and revises the nugget until it is discoverable. We evaluate INO with two production B2B knowledge-assistance agents across multiple companies that use our system: a product support agent that answers questions over company-specific knowledge bases, and a support ticket agent that assists support engineers. INO consistently improves results over baselines in terms of discoverability and usage of factual corrections, in automated and human evaluations.

</details>


### [304] [A Two-Phase Stability Study of LLM Judges and Bar Council Examiners on Thai Bar-Exam Free-Form Essays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25652)
*Pawitsapak Akarajaradwong,Wuttikrai Lertprasertphakorn,Chompakorn Chaksangchaichot,Sarana Nutanong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study evaluates legal essay scoring by comparing 26 language models (LLMs) to three human-expert examiners using a shared dataset. LLMs showed systematic convergence with majority human opinions but failed to replicate minority readings in contested cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether LLMs can match human inter-rater reliability and reproduce nuanced expert disagreements in legal essay evaluations.

Method: Through an identical-inputs protocol, researchers compared scoring from three Bar Council-trained human examiners and 26 LLM judges on 15 candidate answers, analyzing cases with and without clear rubric guidelines.

Result: LLMs generally aligned with majority human opinions, but none reflected minority examiner interpretations accurately. Among LLMs, agreement was systematic rather than split across multiple divergent readings.

Conclusion: The study highlights asymmetric reliability in LLM evaluations. Selection of LLM judges based on human-panel agreement may perpetuate biases, favoring majority over minority interpretations.

Abstract: Free-form legal essay evaluation in NLP treats expert inter-rater stability as a single ceiling number, and treats LLM-judge agreement with that ceiling as evidence of judge stability. We test both assumptions on the Thai bar examination through an identical-inputs protocol: three Bar Council-trained examiners (A, B, C) and a 26-LLM judge panel score the same 15 cross-graded answers from the same four inputs (question, official Bar Council grading regulation, gold answer, candidate answer). The headline finding is asymmetric. On 10 of 15 cells where the rubric prescribes both axes, all 29 raters converge in a tight band: panel agreement is universal. On the remaining 5 cells where the rubric does not prescribe how to grade a correct final answer that omits a decisive statutory citation, the human panel splits between two coherent readings (B/C majority at the upper rubric band, score $6$--$8$; A minority at the lower band, score $1$--$2$). The LLM judge population does not split symmetrically: 22 of 26 LLMs score in or near B/C's contested band, 3 sit in the regulation-silent middle gap, and only 1 (GPT-5.4 Nano) approaches A's band without consistently scoring within it. \emph{Zero LLMs in our 26-judge panel reproduce the minority human reading on the contested cells.} The B/C-direction cluster spans every model size, vendor, and price tier we tested. An instrumented three-LLM anchor sub-panel (Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4 Pro) carries determinism probes, input ablations, and bootstrap CIs, and reaches anchor panel $α= 0.77$ on the 15 cells against human-panel $α= 0.36$. The high LLM-panel $α$ reflects systematic convergence on the majority reading rather than balanced reproduction of both readings; a benchmark that selects its LLM judge by maximising agreement with a human reference panel will inherit this asymmetry by construction.

</details>


### [305] [AutoSG: LLM-Driven Solver Generation Solely from Task Prompts for Expensive Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25658)
*Haoran Gu,Handing Wang,Yi Mei,Mengjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AutoSG is an innovative system that generates customized solvers for expensive optimization tasks automatically using language prompts with validation and efficiency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address key challenges in generating solvers for expensive optimization tasks, including factual inaccuracies, instability during refinement, and high evaluation costs with limited generalization.

Method: The authors propose AutoSG, which incorporates three main innovations: retrieval-augmented generation for code validation, self-refinement for task-specific improvements while maintaining solution structure, and Elo-based evaluation for quick and general solver ranking.

Result: Experiments show that AutoSG significantly outperforms both human-designed frameworks and existing LLM-generated solvers across multiple expensive optimization problems.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that AutoSG offers a reliable, efficient, and superior approach for generating solvers for optimization tasks, marking an advancement in the field of automated problem-solving.

Abstract: Expensive optimization tasks are ubiquitous in real-world applications, demanding highly specialized solvers. While LLM-driven automated solver generation shows promise, current paradigms face three critical issues when tackling expensive optimization: factual hallucinations due to deficient domain knowledge, the frequent dismantling of previously established locally optimal structures during refinement, and the prohibitive evaluation costs alongside restricted generalization caused by executing on training instances. To address these issues, we introduce AutoSG, a fully automated workflow directly translating natural language prompts into executable customized solvers. AutoSG features three core innovations: a retrieval-augmented solver generation module strictly grounding code in verified literature; a one-step self-refinement operator introducing task-specific improvements while preserving critical structural components; and an instance-free Elo-based LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation mechanism rapidly establishing global rankings. Extensive evaluations across diverse expensive optimization tasks confirm AutoSG significantly outperforms human-designed state-of-the-art frameworks and existing LLM-generated solvers.

</details>


### [306] [Llamion Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25676)
*Kisu Yang,Yoonna Jang,Hyeonseok Moon,Hwanseok Jang,Taewoo Lee,Hyungjin Lee,Jeseung Lee,Juhyoung Park,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Llamion is a family of 14B-parameter open-weight language models created by transforming Orion-14B into a standardized Llama-family architecture using the KEPT methodology. These models achieve high performance with minimal computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to create a standardized language model architecture (Llamion) that retains the performance and capabilities of its source model (Orion-14B) while improving usability, efficiency, and adaptability.

Method: The authors use the KEPT recipe, which includes (i) NPM for unchanged modules, (ii) OPM for optimized initialization during transformation, and (iii) XKD to align the converted model's behavior with the source model's.

Result: Llamion matches Orion's behavior on benchmarks such as H6, MT-Bench, and KoMMLU after converting with just ~123M tokens in four days. It also achieves top performance on the Open Ko LLM Leaderboard and retains capabilities like Python programming and long-context handling.

Conclusion: The Llamion models demonstrate that it is possible to standardize and transform large language models into a new architecture while preserving their advanced functionalities and significance, ensuring usability in the Hugging Face Transformers library.

Abstract: We release Llamion, a family of 14B-parameter open-weight language models obtained by transforming Orion-14B into the standardized Llama-family architecture. The transformation is performed by Efficient Knowledge Preservation for Transformation (KEPT), a recipe that combines (i) Normal Parameter Mapping (NPM) for unchanged modules, (ii) Optimized Parameter Mapping (OPM), a training-free LayerNorm-to-RMSNorm initialization we prove optimal under the near-zero-mean activation regime induced by weight decay, and (iii) Cross-architecture Knowledge Distillation (XKD), an equal-size frozen-teacher distillation that aligns the converted model's outputs with the source model's on any reasonable input distribution. Llamion recovers Orion's behaviour on H6, MT-Bench, and KoMMLU with only ~123M tokens on a single A100 in four days; Llamion-Base reaches 66.87% on KoMMLU, exceeding the next-best entry of the Open Ko LLM Leaderboard by >7.0 absolute points at submission time. Capabilities entirely absent from the transfer corpus (Python programming and 200K-token context handling) survive the architectural transition intact. We release three checkpoints (Base, Chat, LongChat) that load with trust_remote_code=False in the Hugging Face Transformers library.

</details>


### [307] [Simulating Human Memory with Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25680)
*Qihan Wang,Nicholas Tomlin,Michael Hu,Brian Dillon,Tal Linzen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines how language models' memory differs from human memory, tests methods to make their memory more human-like, and discusses implications for their use as user simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models are being used increasingly as user simulators, yet their memory exhibits significantly more reliability compared to real human memory, necessitating research to bridge this gap.

Method: The authors run classic memory experiments with both humans and language models, develop prompting strategies and a compactor to adjust memory in models, and evaluate their impact in user-simulation tasks.

Result: The study reveals that unaltered language models have better memory than humans but that specific techniques can make their memory constraints more human-like, improving their role as user simulators.

Conclusion: The research suggests that imposing human-like memory constraints on language models enhances their effectiveness in user simulation, offering substantial benefits for downstream applications like education.

Abstract: Language models are increasingly being deployed as user simulators, but their memory is far more reliable than that of real users. To measure this gap, we run a series of classic memory experiments from psychology on both humans and language models. Across tasks, we find that out-of-the-box language models exhibit better memory than humans, even when prompted to imitate human behavior. We then show that better prompting strategies and the use of a compactor can cause language models to forget content in a more human-like way. Using these methods, we show preliminary evidence that language models with human-like memory constraints can function as more effective user simulators in a downstream education task. Finally, we release human reference data and benchmarks to support future work on simulating human memory with language models.

</details>


### [308] [Testing the Deliteralization Hypothesis in Human and Machine Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25686)
*Malik Marmonier,Rachel Bawden,Benoît Sagot*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines the shift from traditional NMT systems to LLMs in machine translation, focusing on how literal the translations are and if LLMs follow the deliteralization hypothesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate if LLMs contribute to less literal translations and confirm the longstanding deliteralization hypothesis in translation studies.

Method: A Synthetic Literality Index based on six heuristics was used to measure the literality of translations across 54 language pairs and three tasks: direct translation, iterative revision, and post-editing, utilizing both human translators and a mix of NMT systems and LLMs.

Result: LLMs narrow the literality gap between machine translations and human translations, show evidence of deliteralization over iterative revisions, and behave differently when post-editing compared to human editors.

Conclusion: Recent LLMs, while still more literal than human translations, exhibit a capability for deliteralization and suggest a distinct approach to translation revision, reshaping perspectives on machine translation systems.

Abstract: The recent shift from dedicated NMT systems to general-purpose LLMs has reshaped machine translation, with LLMs reported to produce more fluent, less literal output than their predecessors. We test whether this shift extends to the deliteralization hypothesis, the long-standing claim from translation studies that translations become progressively less literal as they are drafted and revised. Using the WMT24++ dataset, we compare the literality of human translations and post-editions to that of two NMT systems and six LLMs across 54 language pairs and three tasks: direct translation, iterative self-revision, and post-editing of human drafts. Literality is measured via a validated Synthetic Literality Index built from six heuristics. We find that (i) human translations remain significantly less literal than those of all tested MT systems, though recent LLMs narrow the gap; (ii) when prompted to iteratively revise their own output, LLMs deliteralize monotonically, providing the first evidence that the hypothesis applies natively to LLM generation; and (iii) as post-editors, LLMs invert the revision triggers of human post-editors, tolerating literal drafts and targeting idiomatic human formulations for revision.

</details>


### [309] [From Facts to Insights: A Persona-Driven Dual Memory Framework and Dataset for Role-Playing Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25693)
*Rongsheng Zhang,Ruofan Hu,Weijie Chen,Jiji Tang,Junnan Ren,Wanying Wu,Xunuoyan Chen,Tangjie Lv,Tao Jin,Zhou Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The authors introduce RoleMemo and the DualMem framework to enhance long-term, persona-consistent role-playing agents, addressing limitations of persona-agnostic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing role-playing agents struggle with long-term interactions as context windows become overwhelmed, and current memory frameworks fail to maintain persona fidelity due to persona-agnostic summarization.

Method: The authors create the RoleMemo dataset with four reasoning tasks that link factual fragments to persona-specific interpretations. They propose the DualMem framework, which splits memory into factual cognition and persona-conditioned insight, trained using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL).

Result: Evaluation shows DualMem, a 4B-parameter model, outperforms persona-agnostic frameworks like DeepSeek-V3.2 in maintaining sustained persona fidelity during long-term conversations.

Conclusion: DualMem and RoleMemo demonstrate the importance of persona-conditioned memory, addressing a key gap in long-term conversational AI while achieving superior results over existing solutions.

Abstract: While role-playing agents excel in short-term interactions, long-term conversations overwhelm context windows, motivating external memory frameworks. Current systems typically rely on persona-agnostic summarization, which records facts without persona-specific interpretation, yielding generic responses that compromise persona fidelity. To bridge this gap, we introduce RoleMemo, a dataset featuring four reasoning tasks where the factual fragments must be interpreted through the persona to reach the correct answer. Evaluation on RoleMemo exposes critical limitations of persona-agnostic frameworks. We thus propose DualMem, which decouples memory into two streams: factual cognition and persona-conditioned insight. Trained through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), our framework with a 4B-parameter model outperforms zero-shot persona-agnostic frameworks powered by DeepSeek-V3.2 for sustained persona fidelity. Our resources are available at https://github.com/role2026/rolememo.

</details>


### [310] [PowLU: An Activation Function for Stable Pre-Training of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25704)
*Peijie Jiang,Yuqi Feng,Cunyin Peng,Qian Zhao,Jia Liu,KunLong Chen,Zhiqiang Zhang,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new activation function, Power Linear Unit (PowLU), to replace SwiGLU in large language models, addressing numerical instability in low-precision training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The existing SwiGLU activation function, commonly used in LLMs, causes numerical instability due to its approximate quadratic amplification when inputs or model scales increase, particularly in low-precision training.

Method: PowLU introduces a rational power function to adaptively manage nonlinearity, enhancing stability and representation abilities in large-scale training of LLMs. Theoretical properties and experimental validations are included.

Result: Scaling law experiments and trials with Ling architectures (7.9B and 124B parameters) show PowLU is competitive with SwiGLU, improves scalability, and maintains consistent performance across various model sizes.

Conclusion: PowLU is a robust and effective activation function, addressing SwiGLU's instability in LLM training while supporting large-scale model scalability and maintaining competitive results.

Abstract: In contemporary large language models (LLMs), the swish-gated linear unit (SwiGLU) activation function is widely adopted to regulate the information flow and introduce non-linearity. For large positive inputs, SwiGLU approximates the quadratic function $x^2$, providing strong nonlinearity and expressive capacity. However, this property also causes numerical instability as the input or model scale increases, particularly in low-precision LLM training. The main reason is its approximate quadratic amplification, which enlarges the output range and exacerbates outliers. To address this issue, we propose a stable activation function, Power Linear Unit (PowLU), for large-scale LLM pre-training. Specifically, PowLU employs a rational power function to achieve adaptive nonlinearity, thereby improving representation ability and enabling stable training in spike regions. Moreover, we provide theoretical justification for several key properties of PowLU. Scaling law experiments confirm that the performance is consistent across model sizes, and further experimental results with the Ling architecture (7.9B and 124B total parameters) demonstrate that PowLU achieves competitive results against SwiGLU and SwiGLU-Clip in large-scale training of LLMs. In addition, the experimental results also show that PowLU effectively improves the scalability of the large-scale training of LLMs.

</details>


### [311] [Trait-Aware Policy Optimization for Autoregressive Multi-Trait Essay Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25731)
*Zhengyang Wang,Sanwoo Lee,Jiaxin Wang,Chenxi Miao,Weikang Li,Yunfang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method called TAPO to improve autoregressive models for multi-trait essay scoring by using tailored post-training frameworks focusing on specific reward dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation was to address the challenge of effectively post-training autoregressive models for scoring essays across multiple traits.

Method: The method involves Trait-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO) that decomposes rewards across sample and trait dimensions and enhances supervised fine-tuning with trait-focused prompts.

Result: Experiments showed that TAPO improved the scoring performance compared to supervised fine-tuning and scalar-reward optimization baselines.

Conclusion: Trait-aware post-training frameworks, such as TAPO, enhance effectiveness and transferability in multi-trait essay scoring tasks.

Abstract: Multi-trait essay scoring aims to provide fine-grained evaluation of writing quality across multiple dimensions. However, how to effectively post-train autoregressive scoring models remains underexplored. In this paper, we propose Trait-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO), a post-training framework tailored to autoregressive multi-trait scoring. Our method decomposes rewards along both the sample and trait dimensions, combining global scoring consistency, trait-level accuracy, format validity, and inter-trait dependency preservation. In addition, we enhance supervised fine-tuning with enhanced prompts, allowing the model to internalize trait semantics before preference optimization. Experiments across multiple backbone models show that our method consistently improves multi-trait scoring performance over supervised fine-tuning and scalar-reward optimization baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness and transferability of trait-aware post-training for essay scoring.

</details>


### [312] [Selective Latent Thinking: Adaptive Compression of LLM Reasoning Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25745)
*Hui Xie,Jie Liu,Ziyue Qiao,Joaquin Vanschore*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Selective Latent Thinking (SLT) improves reasoning efficiency and maintains accuracy by compressing redundant spans into latent representations, while keeping precision-critical spans explicit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models (LLMs) show improved reasoning ability via explicit chain-of-thought reasoning, but this comes at a high computational cost. Efficient latent reasoning methods exist but risk over-compression that degrades accuracy, necessitating a better approach.

Method: SLT selectively compresses redundant reasoning spans into latent forms while preserving precision-critical spans as explicit CoT. It uses a lightweight decoder for span prediction, confidence-based gating for compression reliability, and a three-stage training approach including latent compression, reliability-aware prediction, and trajectory-level reinforcement learning.

Result: Experiments across mathematical reasoning benchmarks show SLT achieves 22.7% higher accuracy than latent reasoning baselines at equal compression ratios, reduces reasoning chain length by 58.4%, with only a 2.8% accuracy loss compared to explicit CoT.

Conclusion: SLT balances accuracy and efficiency by combining latent reasoning with selective explicit reasoning, offering a significant advancement for reasoning-efficient LLMs.

Abstract: Explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning substantially improves the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs), but incurs high inference cost due to lengthy autoregressive traces. Existing latent reasoning methods offer a promising alternative, yet they often treat reasoning as uniformly compressible, causing precision-critical intermediate steps to be overly compressed and thereby degrading reasoning accuracy. In this work, we propose Selective Latent Thinking (SLT), a framework that selectively compresses redundant reasoning spans into latent representations while preserving precision-critical spans as explicit CoT within the same reasoning trajectory. Specifically, SLT first uses a lightweight decoder to anticipate a short upcoming reasoning span, and then applies confidence-based gating to determine the longest span that can be reliably compressed. The accepted span is encoded into a compact latent representation to improve reasoning efficiency, while uncertain or precision-critical reasoning remains in explicit CoT form to preserve accuracy. To learn this selective compression policy, SLT adopts a three-stage training strategy that combines span-level latent compression, reliability-aware future reasoning prediction, and trajectory-level reinforcement learning to optimize the trade-off between answer correctness and reasoning cost. Extensive experiments across four mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SLT achieves 22.7\% higher accuracy than latent reasoning baselines at comparable compression ratios, while reducing reasoning chain length by 58.4\% with only 2.8\% accuracy degradation compared to explicit CoT,Our code can be found in https://github.com/hunshi34/SLT.

</details>


### [313] [StreamProfileBench: A Benchmark for Fine-Grained User Profile Inference in Real-World Streaming Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25758)
*Sizhe Wang,Feiyu Duan,Juelin Wang,Liwen Zhang,Feiyu Duan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces StreamProfileBench, a benchmark for evaluating the ability of LLMs to adapt to continuously evolving user profiles using streaming user-generated content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of user profiling focus on static data and fail to account for the dynamic nature of evolving user profiles, which are essential for personalized systems.

Method: The authors formalize streaming user profiling as a task of continuous state maintenance, curate a dataset with over 120,000 user-generated content posts from diverse platforms, and propose an annotation-free evaluation framework that captures temporal correlations in user interests.

Result: Experiments with 14 leading LLMs show that these models struggle with continuous profile updates, exhibiting biases like retaining outdated interests and failing to capture interest decay.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for improved methodologies in continuous user profiling and validates the importance of incorporating streaming paradigms into evaluation benchmarks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have reshaped user profiling, yet current evaluations mainly focus on static data snapshots. This paradigm overlooks the reality of personalized systems, where User-Generated Content (UGC) arrives continuously and fine-grained profile evolve rapidly. To bridge this gap, we introduce StreamProfileBench, a large-scale benchmark for fine-grained streaming user profiling. We formalize streaming user profiling as a continuous state maintenance task and curate a highly authentic dataset comprising over 120,000 UGC posts from 7,000+ real users across five diverse platforms. By leveraging the temporal correlation of user interests, we further propose a novel, annotation-free evaluation framework. Extensive experiments across 14 leading LLMs reveal that continuous profile updating remains an open challenge. Models exhibit a systemic conservative bias, over-retaining past interests while failing to recognize interest decay. Ablation experiments further validate the practical utility and necessity of the streaming paradigm.

</details>


### [314] [Double Triangle Annotation: A Scalable Human-in-the-Loop Framework for High-Precision Historical Document Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25781)
*Yi Ren*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a high-precision, semi-automated annotation framework for extracting structured information from historical documents, leveraging human intervention only for cases of model disagreement and achieving excellent accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Creating high-precision ground-truth annotations for information extraction from historical documents is challenging due to the cost of manual labeling and the unreliability of automated pipelines prone to hallucination.

Method: The authors introduce a two-layer human-in-the-loop system called Double Triangle Annotation. It uses cross-consensus from two different multimodal large language models for automated annotation. Disagreements are resolved by human intervention in a layered manner, with domain experts handling residual conflicts.

Result: In the context of the Guides Rosenwald historical medical corpus, the framework achieved a Word Error Rate of 0.003. It auto-accepted over 85% of the annotations, significantly reducing the need for manual intervention.

Conclusion: The proposed annotation framework ensures high-precision outcomes using cross-model consensus and human review only for edge cases. It offers a scalable solution for generating benchmarks for historical document processing.

Abstract: Evaluating structured-information extraction from historical documents at scale requires high-precision ground-truth annotations, yet traditional manual labeling is expensive and fully automated pipelines built on large language models are prone to hallucination. We propose Double Triangle Annotation, a two-layer human-in-the-loop framework that leverages cross-model consensus to automate the majority of annotation work while ensuring high-precision outputs. In the first layer, two architecturally independent Multimodal Large Language Models annotate each document in parallel; when they agree, the label is auto-accepted, and disagreements are routed to a human jury. A second layer cross-checks two such systems against each other, escalating residual conflicts to a domain expert. The framework rests on a single assumption -- error independence between models -- requires no distributional priors or task-specific calibration, and becomes more autonomous as model capability improves. On the Guides Rosenwald, a corpus of French medical directories spanning 1887-1906, the framework achieves a final Word Error Rate of 0.003. Applied at scale, model consensus auto-accepts over 85% of 13,595 fields. We release the resulting benchmark -- the first structured-extraction ground truth for the Rosenwald Guides -- to support future work on historical document processing.

</details>


### [315] [Adaptive Graph Refinement and Label Propagation with LLMs for Cost-Effective Entity Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25814)
*Hongtao Wang,Renchi Yang,Haoran Zheng,Xiangyu Ke*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Alper, a framework for dirty entity resolution, which unifies matching and clustering steps to dynamically and iteratively improve entity graphs while using cost-effective optimization techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The traditional blocking-matching-clustering paradigm for entity resolution has critical flaws, such as error propagation due to decoupled steps and suboptimal clustering caused by rigid transitivity.

Method: The paper proposes a unified framework, Alper, that integrates matching and clustering into an iterative probabilistic label propagation process. It dynamically refines the graph by combining weak signals from graph propagation and strong signals from expensive LLM-based pairwise queries.

Result: Experimental results on eight benchmark datasets show that Alper outperforms existing cascaded pipelines in terms of effectiveness and cost-efficiency.

Conclusion: Alper demonstrates how integrating matching and clustering steps with adaptive signal selection and optimization can lead to superior performance in entity resolution compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: Dirty entity resolution (ER), which identifies records referring to the same real-world entity from a single, messy dataset, is a fundamental task in data management and mining. However, the dominant blocking-matching-clustering paradigm for ER suffers from critical flaws. Its cascaded, decoupled workflow essentially produces a static, sparse graph plagued by missing edges (due to blocking failures) and noisy links (due to matching errors), causing error propagation and yielding suboptimal clusters, particularly when rigid transitivity is imposed in the clustering. We contend that matching and clustering are fundamentally synergistic, both optimizing for the construction of an ideal entity graph. Building upon this insight, we propose Alper, a unified framework that integrates these steps into an iterative probabilistic label propagation process over a global, evolving graph. Unlike disjoint blocking, Alper refines the graph structure and labels dynamically by adaptively integrating "weak but cheap" signals from graph propagation with "strong but expensive" LLM-based pairwise queries. For higher cost-effectiveness, we formulate the signal selection as a constrained optimization problem maximizing cumulative marginal gain under a query budget, solved via our greedy algorithm with provable theoretical guarantees. Our extensive experiments over eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that Alper is consistently superior to state-of-the-art cascaded pipelines.

</details>


### [316] [Fine-Tuning Over Architectural Complexity: Broad-Coverage PII Detection on PIIBench with DeBERTa](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25816)
*Pritesh Jha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study focuses on enhancing PII detection using multi-source fine-tuning with DeBERTa-based models, achieving significant F1 improvements compared to prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of PII detection systems trained on narrow domain boundaries and improve coverage on heterogeneous text.

Method: The researchers fine-tuned DeBERTa-based models on a multi-source dataset spanning 82 PII entity types, employing three methods: direct fine-tuning, a hierarchical source-conditioned model (SC+H), and a curriculum-learning extension (SC+H+Curr).

Result: Direct fine-tuning outperforms all other methods with an F1 score of 0.6476 on a 5,000-record test set and sustains high performance in a larger 100,002-record evaluation, surpassing previously published models.

Conclusion: Simple direct fine-tuning of models on diverse, task-specific datasets with weighted cross-entropy objectives is more effective for broad-coverage PII detection than introducing architectural or curriculum-based complexity.

Abstract: Personally identifiable information (PII) detection systems are frequently trained within narrow source or domain boundaries, limiting coverage when deployed on heterogeneous text. We study model fine-tuning on a corrected multi-source PIIBench preparation spanning 82 retained entity types across ten source datasets. We evaluate three DeBERTa-based approaches: direct token classification fine-tuning, a source-conditioned hierarchical model (SC+H), and a three-phase curriculum extension (SC+H+Curr). Against eight published comparator systems on a reproducible 5,000-record held-out subset (test_5k), direct fine-tuned DeBERTa achieves F1 0.6476, while SC+H and the curriculum variant achieve 0.5899 and 0.2772 respectively; the strongest published comparator reaches only 0.1723. Because validation initially favoured SC+H, we perform a final streamed evaluation on the complete 100,002-record held-out split. Direct fine-tuning remains superior, achieving F1 0.6455 versus 0.5894 for SC+H.
