We close it with three additive mechanisms: (1) a small, offline-signed clearance assertion a server publishes at a well-known Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) and a host verifies against a pinned trust root before any tool dispatch; (2) a deny-by-default per-server tool allowlist, so admitting a server is not trusting its every tool; and (3) a flavor-gated enforcement mode that turns the checks from warnings into hard denials, with every decision written to a tamper-evident audit log. We give the wire format, the verification algorithm, a security analysis, and an LLM-driven adversarial evaluation; we then state the design in normative Request-for-Comments (RFC 2119) form -- schema, verification rules, error registry, well-known registration, and machine-checkable conformance vectors -- so it can be adopted as an MCP addendum rather than reinvented. An unextended host ignores the well-known document and behaves exactly as today.

</details>


### [892] [AI-Driven Adaptive Adversaries and the Erosion of Cryptographic Trust in Public Key Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24542)
*Petar Radanliev*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper explores how AI-driven adversarial optimization can undermine Public Key Cryptography (PKC) by targeting implementation-level vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the increasing gap between theoretical cryptographic models and real-world operational threats posed by adaptive adversaries exploiting implementation flaws.

Method: The paper likely uses an analytical approach to study how adaptive adversarial optimization exploits PKC vulnerabilities, accounting for implementation-level observability.

Result: Findings reveal that AI-powered adversaries can compromise PKC without breaking underlying cryptographic primitives, highlighting critical weaknesses in current security models.

Conclusion: The research concludes that cryptographic security needs to evolve beyond algorithm-centric models to address operational adversarial realities effectively.

Abstract: This paper examines the erosion of Public Key Cryptography (PKC) security under adaptive adversarial optimisation driven by artificial intelligence. The problem addressed is the growing mismatch between algorithm-centric cryptographic security models and operational attack realities, where adversaries exploit implementation-level observability rather than breaking cryptographic primitives.

</details>


### [893] [KYA: A Framework-Agnostic Trust Layer for Autonomous Systems with Verifiable Provenance and Hierarchical Policy Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25376)
*Kolawole Quadri*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This research presents KYA (Know Your Agents), a trust and governance system for autonomous agents, focusing on ensuring system accuracy and integrity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in detecting issues such as agent errors, drifting, and malicious behavior in autonomous systems and offer a robust governance layer.

Method: They designed KYA with five key primitives, including secure pipelines, hierarchical trust scoring, auditable interaction amplifications, and delegation attribution across 22 agent frameworks, ensuring high scalability and performance.

Result: KYA performs with sub-millisecond latency, rejects invalid inputs with 100% accuracy, and detects 89% of adversarial probes from known attacks like PyRIT and Garak.

Conclusion: KYA provides effective, scalable, and framework-agnostic trust governance for autonomous agents, with open-source availability on PyPI for broader adoption.

Abstract: Observability tells operators when an agent is slow. KYA tells operators when an agent is wrong, drifting, leaking, or quietly going rogue. We present KYA (Know Your Agents), an open-source trust and governance layer for autonomous systems composed of five primitives: (1) a four-gate inbound apply pipeline composing Ed25519 signature verification with multi-anchor pinning, persist-time expiry, only-tighten composition, and operator-approval-as-default; (2) an only-tighten composition algebra over a three-channel multi-tenant hierarchy (platform default,tenant override, signed external recommendation); (3) KYP -- Know Your Principal, a schema-level unification of trust scoring across human users, AI agents, and service accounts; (4) auditable interaction-multiplier amplification over an AIVSS-shaped additive baseline, with bounded asymmetric per-interaction multipliers carrying stable audit codes; and (5) two-axis delegation attribution combining a static observation-gated delegation-trust premium with zero-config runtime orchestrator-blame at three SDK hook surfaces. KYA is framework-agnostic across 22 agent frameworks. The pure-function scorer runs sub-millisecond at p99 and the system sustains ~1,800 ops/sec at 20 concurrent workers with HMAC chain integrity preserved end-to-end. The four-gate inbound apply pipeline rejects forged, expired, loosening, and unapproved recommendations on every trial (1,200 / 1,200) with sub-millisecond p99 latency on SQLite. KYA detects 89% of 1,200 adversarial probes from PyRIT and Garak, including the recently-published topology-guided multi-agent attack. The system is available under Apache 2.0 as the veldt-kya package on PyPI (release candidate at submission time; stable v0.1.0 forthcoming)

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [894] [Methodology for Creating a Clinically Verified Dermoscopic Image Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25168)
*Kozachok Elena Sergeevna*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The study introduces a clinically verified dataset of dermatoscopic images to support medical informatics research, emphasizing structured metadata, reproducible acquisition, and accurate diagnostic labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for this research stems from the need for reliable and reproducible datasets in automated diagnostic systems, addressing gaps related to differing practices in Russia and globally and ensuring diagnostic accuracy.

Method: The methodology involves (1) establishing SOPs for image acquisition using mobile dermatoscopy, (2) creating an information model with 16 structured metadata fields, and (3) a multi-stage expert diagnostic verification process, including histological confirmation for malignant cases.

Result: A pilot dataset of 1,026 unique dermatoscopic images from 443 patients was developed, covering nine nosological categories. Malignant cases (39 in total) were histologically confirmed, and metadata includes detailed annotations with consensus diagnostic levels.

Conclusion: The resulting dataset serves as a clinically validated resource to evaluate diagnostic models, analyze domain shifts, explore interpretability, and support further research advancements.

Abstract: This study presents a methodology for constructing a clinically verified dataset of dermatoscopic images for medical informatics research. The relevance of the work is driven by the fact that the performance of automated diagnostic support systems depends not only on the volume of images, but also on the reproducibility of the image acquisition procedure, the completeness of structured metadata, and the reliability of diagnostic labels. International collections were primarily created under conditions that differ substantially from routine Russian outpatient practice and mobile dermatoscopy. The proposed methodology integrates three interconnected components: (1) a standard operating procedure (SOP) for acquiring images via mobile dermatoscopy, (2) an information model comprising 16 structured metadata fields organized into six clinically oriented blocks in ISIC-compatible notation, and (3) a multi-stage expert verification of diagnostic labels (initial clinical annotation, consensus review by three specialists, and histological confirmation of all malignant neoplasms). Using this methodology, a dataset of 1,026 unique dermatoscopic images from 443 patients was collected between June 2025 and May 2026. From 1,044 initial records, 18 duplicates were excluded. The dataset includes nine nosological categories; all 39 malignant lesions (18 melanomas, 15 basal cell carcinomas, and 6 squamous cell carcinomas) were histologically verified. Patient age ranged from 2 to 90 years (median 38), with 279 females (63%) and 164 males (37%). Each image is accompanied by expert-annotated dermatoscopic structures and an explicit verification_stage field indicating the level of diagnostic confirmation. The resulting dataset serves as a pilot clinically verified resource suitable for independent model evaluation, domain shift analysis, interpretability studies, and further expansion.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [895] [TIP: A Decentralized Intent-Based Protocol for Declarative IoT Interoperability and Sandboxed Schema Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25332)
*Yeison David Mejia Mosquera*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper introduces TIP, a decentralized, declarative network protocol solving IoT heterogeneity through intent-based routing, dynamic schema translation, and strong security measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT systems face challenges due to fragmentation across hardware, networking, and data formats. Traditional address-bound routing limits flexibility, necessitating a more adaptable communication framework for heterogeneous IoT ecosystems.

Method: The paper proposes TIP, a protocol that replaces physical endpoint addressing with abstract intents, hybrid discovery (mDNS + Kademlia DHT), dynamic data representation translation (via WebAssembly sandboxes), and multi-criteria node selection. It incorporates advanced cryptographic security measures.

Result: The TIP implementation, written in Rust and C++, achieves sub-millisecond translation overhead and demonstrates high resilience, making it viable under demanding industrial conditions.

Conclusion: TIP successfully addresses IoT system heterogeneity, enabling dynamic compatibility, flexibility, and security through a decentralized, intent-based protocol.

Abstract: Heterogeneous Internet of Things (IoT) systems suffer from fragmentation across hardware architectures, networking stacks, and data serialization formats. Existing standards (such as MQTT, COAP, and DDS) rely on address-bound, imperative routing models that require hardcoded configurations and leave no flexibility for runtime schema translation. This paper presents TIP (The Intent Protocol), a decentralized, declarative network protocol. Instead of addressing specific physical endpoints, nodes submit abstract intents specifying desired capabilities, schemas, and Quality of Service (QoS) constraints. The TIP Engine resolves matching nodes using a hybrid discovery mechanism combining local multicast DNS (mDNS) with Kademlia Distributed Hash Tables (DHT). Selection is optimized via a multi-criteria scoring algorithm incorporating network latency, historical reputation, and contract compliance. Mismatched data representations are reconciled on-the-fly inside isolated WebAssembly (WASM) sandboxes compiled dynamically from TOML specifications. Security is enforced through Ed25519 signatures, X25519 key exchanges, and ChaCha20-Poly 1305 payload encryption. Evaluation of our reference implementation in Rust and C++ shows sub-millisecond translation overhead and robust resilience under industrial conditions.

</details>


<div id='nlin.AO'></div>

# nlin.AO [[Back]](#toc)

### [896] [Memory Uncertainty Relation and Harmonic Memory in Random Recurrent Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24628)
*Taichi Haruna,Kohei Nakajima*

Main category: nlin.AO

TL;DR: The paper introduces an inequality providing a lower bound on the short-term memory of dynamical systems, explores its validity under various conditions, and examines the phenomenon of noise-induced memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to clarify how input-driven state fluctuations influence the short-term memory in dynamical systems and to establish foundational principles governing these interactions.

Method: The study derives a memory inequality based on state fluctuation measures, investigates it using analytical and numerical approaches across different reservoir systems under noise, and incorporates state-space regularization to refine understanding.

Result: The research demonstrates scenarios where the inequality holds exactly or asymptotically, identifies noise-induced memory under certain conditions, and finds limitations of the memory uncertainty relation when considering regularized and harmonic memory.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a novel perspective by showing that input noise and state-space regularization can significantly modify memory behavior in dynamical systems, highlighting both the utility and constraints of the proposed inequality.

Abstract: We present an inequality that bounds the short-term memory capability of dynamical systems from below. It can be interpreted as an uncertainty relation between a measure of short-term memory and that of the size of state fluctuations induced by input signals. The lower bound can be achieved by a readout weight and thus represents a suboptimal memory called harmonic memory. We examine analytically and numerically the inequality in a number of reservoir systems subject to input noise. We illustrate cases in which equality is achieved exactly, equality holds asymptotically, and the inequality is strict. We also study the effect of a state-space regularization to elucidate the inequality in terms of the fluctuation structure of the state-space. We find that a certain strength of input noise induces extra memory under the regularization, and we refer to this phenomenon as noise-induced memory. We observe that the memory uncertainty relation does not hold in general for the regularized memory and harmonic memory. This fact is explained in terms of the mechanism of noise-induced memory.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [897] [On the Sample Complexity of Robust Binary Hypothesis Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24741)
*Shankar Vallinayagam,Ankit Pensia,Varun Jog*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper investigates the robustness of binary hypothesis testing under three contamination models and explores their sample complexity under various conditions. It derives findings on the instability and relationships of sample complexities across these models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to analyze how different contamination models affect the stability and behavior of sample complexity in binary hypothesis testing, a crucial aspect in maintaining reliable statistical inference under adversarial conditions.

Method: The authors analyze and compare three contamination models: ε-additive (Huber), ε-subtractive, and ε-total variation (TV). They derive explicit formulas, examine stability with respect to contamination parameters, identify least favorable distributions, and establish constant-factor comparability across models.

Result: The study demonstrates that sample complexity in all three contamination models is highly sensitive to the contamination parameter ε. Explicit formulas for least favorable distributions are provided, and the sample complexities across models are shown to be comparable within constant-factor rescaling of ε. Results also extend to adaptive contamination models.

Conclusion: Despite the instability issues, the three contamination models are shown to behave similarly in terms of sample complexity scaling with ε, making it possible to draw insights about robustness under adversarial contamination scenarios and adapt to different models.

Abstract: We study the sample complexity of robust binary hypothesis testing under three standard contamination models: $\varepsilon$-additive (Huber), $\varepsilon$-subtractive, and $\varepsilon$-total variation (TV), denoted by $n^*_{\mathrm{Hub}}(\varepsilon)$, $n^*_{\mathrm{Sub}}(\varepsilon)$, and $n^*_{\mathrm{TV}}(\varepsilon)$, respectively. For subtractive contamination, we show that least favourable distributions exist and provide explicit formulas for the same, bringing this model in line with the classical Huber and TV models. Next we show that in all three models, sample complexity may be highly unstable in the contamination parameter $\varepsilon$, increasing by polynomial factors even for $o(\varepsilon)$ perturbations. Similarly, there may be polynomial factor gaps between the sample complexities when $\varepsilon$ is known exactly versus when it is known up to $o(\varepsilon)$ error. Despite the instability of the sample complexity in all models, we show that the sample complexities across models are comparable up to constant-factor rescaling of $\varepsilon$. Specifically, for any fixed $δ_0>0$, the following hold for all distributions $p$ and $q$: (i) $n^*_{\mathrm{Hub}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim n^*_{\mathrm{TV}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim n^*_{\mathrm{Hub}}(2\varepsilon)$, (ii) $n^*_{\mathrm{Sub}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim n^*_{\mathrm{TV}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim n^*_{\mathrm{Sub}}((2+δ_0)\varepsilon)$, and (iii) $n^*_{\mathrm{Sub}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim n^*_{\mathrm{Hub}}(\varepsilon) \lesssim
  n^*_{\mathrm{Sub}}((1+δ_0)\varepsilon)$, and the scaling constants are tight. Finally, we extend our results to adaptive versions of the contamination models.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [898] [Different Statistical Perspectives for Understanding Generalisation in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25452)
*Nil Ayday,Mahalakshmi Sabanayagam,Debarghya Ghoshdastidar*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper explores three mathematical frameworks to understand the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): learning theory, infinite-width/asymptotics approximations, and random graph models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance and prediction capabilities of GNNs are widely recognized across various applications, but there is limited mathematical understanding of their statistical generalization and theoretical underpinnings.

Method: The study categorizes and analyzes GNNs using three key perspectives: 1) learning theory and hypothesis complexity, 2) infinite asymptotics (e.g., Gaussian processes, kernel approximations), and 3) insights from random graph models like stochastic block models.

Result: The paper highlights key theoretical results, shedding light on generalization bounds, expressivity, and error rates of GNNs derived from the three frameworks.

Conclusion: The paper identifies limitations and open research questions for each mathematical framework, calling for further study to deepen the theoretical understanding of GNNs.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNN) are currently the most popular approach for learning and prediction on graph-structured data and are deployed in various fields, from social network analysis to drug discovery. However, there is limited mathematical understanding of the performance of GNNs. We discuss the various perspectives used to study statistical generalisation in GNNs. We identify three broad frameworks. The first approach, rooted in learning theory, relies on uniform convergence bounds and the complexity of the hypothesis class of specific GNN architectures. This approach also builds on the expressivity of GNNs, typically studied through the lens of graph isomorphism tests. The second principle is to simplify the neural architecture by analysing GNNs under the asymptotics of infinitely many parameters or infinite graph size. This approach approximates GNNs using Gaussian processes, neural tangent kernels or graphon neural network operators, which allow studying the generalisation or stability of trained GNNs. The third framework studies GNNs under random graph models, often the contextual stochastic block model, and derives non-asymptotic error rates using tools from high-dimensional statistics. We highlight some key theoretical results and discuss a few limitations and open research questions for each perspective.

</details>


### [899] [Geometry Adaptive Counterfactual Distribution Learning with Diffusion-Guided Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25811)
*Kwangho Kim*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces diffusion-guided estimators for counterfactual distribution learning to address high-dimensional outcome challenges, significantly reducing errors through geometry-adaptive smoothing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual distribution learning struggles with high-dimensional outcomes when the data concentrates in lower-dimensional forms, leading to inefficiencies with isotropic smoothing approaches.

Method: The authors propose two estimators: diffusion-informed smoothing for densities and diffusion-informed score smoothing. These estimators use semiparametric debiasing and geometry-adaptive localization to improve outcome analysis.

Result: Asymptotic expansions, risk bounds, and inference procedures were developed, showing that errors are governed by an effective diffusion-induced dimension rather than the ambient dimension. Experimental validation with CelebA demonstrated improved performance.

Conclusion: The proposed diffusion-guided estimators align smoothing with local geometry, enabling more accurate and computationally efficient counterfactual distribution learning for structured high-dimensional data.

Abstract: We study counterfactual distribution learning for high-dimensional outcomes whose counterfactual law may concentrate near lower-dimensional structure. Standard isotropic smoothing treats all ambient directions equally, leading to unfavorable scaling and unstable local inference. We propose two diffusion-guided estimators based on semiparametric debiasing: diffusion-informed smoothing for counterfactual densities and diffusion-informed score smoothing for counterfactual scores. The estimators combine causal nuisance adjustment with geometry-adaptive localization driven by diffusion score information, removing first-order nuisance bias while aligning smoothing with local outcome geometry. We establish asymptotic expansions, risk bounds, and inference procedures for smoothed density and score-based targets, with ambient density inference obtained under additional approximation conditions. Under structural geometry conditions, the leading stochastic error is governed by an effective dimension induced by the diffusion-guided kernel, rather than by the ambient dimension. Semi-synthetic experiments based on CelebA show steeper error decay for geometry-adaptive methods, supporting the proposed effective-dimension theory.

</details>


### [900] [High-Dimensional Change-Point Detection via Angular Kernel Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25855)
*Jyotishka Ray Choudhury,Yao Xie*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method for change-point detection in high-dimensional, low sample size data optimized for heavy-tailed or contaminated distributions, and proves its reliability through theoretical guarantees and simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for robust change-point detection in high-dimensional settings where only small batches of data are available, particularly under challenging conditions such as heavy-tailed or contaminated distributions.

Method: The authors developed a dimension-averaged angular kernel scan framework to detect distributional shifts in high-dimensional data. It aggregates angular discrepancies across dimensions, is nonparametric, and does not rely on marginal moment assumptions. The framework includes offline and sequential extensions, with statistical guarantees for calibration, type-I error control, power, and localization.

Result: The method achieves a local detection scale of $d^{-1/2}$, ensures accurate detection and localization, and delivers theoretical guarantees, such as ARL calibration and power bounds, even under difficult HDLSS and streaming scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is theoretically sound and effective in detecting changes in high-dimensional data, outperforming moment-based or hyperparameter-sensitive approaches in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: We study change-point detection for high-dimensional data in regimes where inference must be performed from small batches of observations. Our primary focus is the high-dimensional, low sample size (HDLSS) regime, where the sequence length is fixed while the ambient dimension diverges. We propose a dimension-averaged angular kernel scan framework for detecting marginal distributional shifts. The statistic aggregates bounded one-dimensional angular discrepancies across coordinates, yielding a fully nonparametric, hyperparameter-free, and moment-agnostic estimator that remains well-defined without specifying, estimating, or assuming finite marginal moments, for example under heavy-tailed or contaminated distributions. For the offline single-change problem, we derive an exact population mean factorization into a universal deterministic shape function and a scalar signal factor, characterize the null covariance structure up to a scalar long-run variance factor, and establish an HDLSS multivariate central limit theorem under cross-coordinate mixing. These results lead to plug-in Gaussian calibration, asymptotic type-I error control, and power and localization guarantees, including a $d^{-1/2}$ local detection scale. We further extend the offline procedure to a fixed-window sequential monitoring procedure for high-dimensional streaming data, and obtain ARL calibration and worst-case EDD bounds. Simulation studies demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately detect and localize changes in challenging HDLSS and streaming settings where moment-based or hyperparameter-sensitive procedures may be unreliable.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [901] [Algorithms with Polynomially-Improved Approximation Factors for the $2 \rightarrow q$ Norm, and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25303)
*Samuel B. Hopkins,Stefan Tiegel*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper introduces polynomial-time algorithms for approximating the $2 \rightarrow q$ matrix norm (for $q > 2$), achieving better results than prior methods. It offers practical benefits in combinatorial optimization, quantum information, and statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to improve the approximation of the $2 \rightarrow q$ matrix norm, an important problem with downstream applications in optimization, quantum computing, and statistics, where prior methods had limited success or assumptions.

Method: The authors develop polynomial-time approximation algorithms and leverage sum-of-squares certificates to surpass existing baselines for the $2 \rightarrow q$ norm. A concrete improvement is made for $q=4$, achieving a $d^{1/8}$-approximation.

Result: The paper achieves the first polynomial-time algorithm outperforming the $d^{1/4}$-approximation baseline for approximating the $2 \rightarrow q$ norm, specifically developing better results for $q=4$.

Conclusion: These results mark a breakthrough by beating previously unachievable approximation baselines, with implications for robust data analysis, clustering, and other computational tasks.

Abstract: The $2 \rightarrow q$ norm of a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is defined as $\lVert X \rVert_{2 \rightarrow q} = \sup_{\lVert v \rVert_2 = 1} \lVert Xv \rVert_q$. We give polynomial-time multiplicative approximation algorithms for this norm when $q > 2$ (i.e. in the hypercontractive setting). This problem either directly captures or is closely related to long-standing open problems in combinatorial optimization and hardness of approximation (e.g. Small Set Expansion), quantum information (e.g. Best Separable State), and algorithmic statistics.
  Very little is known about what approximation factors we can achieve for this problem in polynomial time, even though such approximations have significant downstream consequences. Barak, Brandão, Harrow, Kelner, Steurer, and Zhou showed that no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve an approximation factor better than $2^{\sqrt{\log n}}$, assuming the Exponential Time Hypothesis (FOCS'12). On the other hand, a simple spectral algorithm gives a $d^{1/4}$-approximation as a baseline. We give, to the best of our knowledge, the first polynomial-time approximation algorithm beating this baseline by polynomial factors. For the important special case of $q = 4$ it achieves a $d^{1/8}$-approximation. All previous algorithms required additional assumptions on $X$, or only surpassed the baseline for small values of $n$.
  Moreover, we construct sum-of-squares certificates for the $2 \rightarrow q$ norm. This directly implies improved algorithms for robust mean and covariance estimation, robust regression, and clustering, when the data only satisfies a bound on its $q$-th moment.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [902] [Uncovering multi-channel magnetic hopfion annihilation via a single-node, billion-spin-scale atomistic framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26016)
*Qichen Xu,Anna Delin*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: SpinX is a GPU-native framework designed for atomistic spin simulations with high performance and flexibility, validated through various tests and applied to study annihilation pathways of magnetic hopfions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing computational cost of atomistic spin simulations for large 3D magnetic textures necessitates efficient and scalable computational frameworks capable of handling complex spin Hamiltonian evaluations.

Method: SpinX leverages a unified Hamiltonian interface, crystallographic sublattice decomposition, and computational backends such as dense, sparse, and FFT-based techniques. It is implemented in JAX and supports various simulation modes including Monte Carlo sampling, dynamical spectroscopy, and transition-path calculations.

Result: Performance benchmarks show SpinX achieving over 10 billion spin-site operations per second on a single accelerator and handling workloads of over 1 billion atomic spins. The framework validated its accuracy through tests on analytical single-spin dynamics, thermodynamics, and dynamic structure factors.

Conclusion: SpinX provides an efficient and scalable tool for atomistic spin simulations, enabling new insights such as discovering competing annihilation pathways in magnetic hopfions on atomistic scales.

Abstract: Modern atomistic spin simulations combine long stochastic trajectories, thermodynamic sampling, static optimization and multi-image transition-path workflows, all of which rely on repeated evaluation of spin Hamiltonians and become computationally prohibitive on the large lattices required for three-dimensional magnetic textures. We introduce SpinX, a GPU-native atomistic spin simulation framework built around a unified Hamiltonian interface and multiple user-selectable computational backends. Its core is a crystallographic sublattice decomposition that reformulates translationally invariant spin interactions as multi-channel tensor convolutions, enabling dense, sparse and FFT-based convolution backends, while irregular systems are handled by pair-list evaluation and long-range dipolar fields by reciprocal-space FFT. Implemented in JAX, SpinX supports deterministic and stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics, Monte Carlo sampling, static optimization, dynamical spectroscopy and string and geodesic nudged elastic band transition-path calculations on heterogeneous accelerator platforms. A validated mixed-precision mode combines fp32 field evaluation with fp64 spin-state propagation. We validate SpinX against analytical single-spin dynamics, finite-size thermodynamics of bcc Fe and transverse dynamic structure factors. Performance benchmarks show peak throughput exceeding 10 billion spin-site operations per second on a single accelerator and aggregate single-node workloads of over 1 billion atomic spins. Applying this framework to an exchange-stabilized magnetic hopfion, we uncover two competing annihilation channels on a million-spin atomistic lattice: a previously reported axial-collapse pathway and a distinct lateral-rupture pathway with a different transition morphology and activation barrier.(Due to arXiv's limit, the abstract shown here is a shortened version)

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [903] [Quadratically Regularized Optimal Transport: Localization Bounds and Affine Case Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24644)
*Long Nguyen-Chi,Nam Nguyen,Binh Nguyen*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper investigates the localization rate of Quadratic Optimal Transport (QOT) optimizers around the Monge coupling and establishes theoretical bounds on their concentration and sparsity properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand the behavior of QOT optimizers, particularly their localization and sparsity, which promise advantages over entropic regularization in computational optimal transport.

Method: The authors derive mathematical lower bounds for the localization rate using Hausdorff distance and analyze the mean squared deviations in the QOT setup. They also validate their findings with synthetic experiments in high-dimensional settings.

Result: The paper shows that the support of the QOT optimizer concentrates no faster than order $\varepsilon^{1/(d+2)}$, and the QOT value gap controls mean-squared deviation by $\varepsilon^{2/(d+2)}$. They also provide a sharp tube bound for the affine Brenier regime.

Conclusion: These results confirm theoretical predictions about the sparsity and localization properties of QOT, advancing understanding of its application as an alternative to entropic regularization. Validation through synthetic high-dimensional examples strengthens the theoretical claims.

Abstract: Quadratic regularization has emerged as a potential alternative to the popular entropic regularization in computational optimal transport, offering the theoretical advantage of producing sparse couplings through its hinge density structure. Despite recent progress in one-dimensional settings and general upper bounds, fundamental questions about the localization rate of QOT optimizers around the Monge coupling have remained open. In this work, we establish a general lower bound showing that the support of the QOT optimizer cannot concentrate around the Monge graph faster than order $\varepsilon^{\frac{1}{d+2}}$ in the directed Hausdorff distance, matching the conjectured optimal exponent under standard regularity assumptions in \citet{wiesel2025sparsity}. We also show that the QOT value gap controls the mean-squared deviation $\mathbb E_{π_\varepsilon}\|y-T(x)\|^2$ by the scale of $\varepsilon^{\frac{2}{d+2}}$. As a corollary, in the affine Brenier regime, which includes Gaussian-to-Gaussian transport, we derive a sharp pointwise tube bound of order $\varepsilon^{\frac{1}{d+2}}$ by reducing the problem to self-transport and applying recent self-transport sparsity results. Finally, we validate our theoretical bound with a synthetic experiment in high-dimensional settings.

</details>


### [904] [Boosted Stochastic Frank-Wolfe for Constrained Nonconvex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25255)
*Navil Nandhan,Abbas Khademi,Antonio Silveti-Falls*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new step size strategy for the boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm to make it applicable in the stochastic setting. The approach maintains convergence rate guarantees and demonstrates better performance in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of the previous boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm, which required step sizes dependent on line search or Lipschitz constant, and to extend its utility to stochastic and nonconvex problems.

Method: The authors propose a novel step size strategy that does not rely on the Lipschitz constant of the gradient. They extend the boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm to stochastic settings and provide theoretical convergence guarantees for various gradient estimators.

Result: The enhanced algorithm works with different gradient estimators (e.g., SAGA, L-SVRG) and retains convergence rates. It also provides new analysis for nonconvex and quasar-convex objectives. Experiments demonstrate faster convergence.

Conclusion: The proposed stochastic boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm is both theoretically and empirically validated to outperform traditional stochastic and non-boosted algorithms in convergence metrics, making it practically valuable for applications like sparse logistic regression.

Abstract: The boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm accelerates the classical Frank-Wolfe algorithm by better aligning the update direction with the negative gradient. Its analysis, however, has been limited to deterministic convex problems, with step sizes that require either line search or knowledge of the Lipschitz constant of the gradient. We develop a novel step size strategy that does not depend on the Lipschitz constant of the gradient, which allows us to extend the boosted Frank-Wolfe algorithm to the stochastic setting. We prove that boosting with this step size strategy can be combined with many modern gradient estimators, including SAGA, L-SVRG, SAG, Heavy Ball momentum, and zeroth-order estimators, among others, while retaining the worst-case convergence rates of ordinary stochastic Frank-Wolfe. Our analysis also yields the first convergence rates for boosted Frank-Wolfe on nonconvex and quasar-convex objectives, results which are new even for deterministic problems. Experiments on sparse logistic regression and quantum process tomography show that stochastic boosted Frank-Wolfe achieves faster convergence per gradient oracle call (and on wall-clock) compared to the non-boosted baseline.

</details>


### [905] [Lifted Schrödinger Bridges for Gaussian Mixture Endpoints: Projection Gaps and Path-Space Obstructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24795)
*Siddhartha Ganguly,George Rapakoulias,Panagiotis Tsiotras*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper investigates optimizing stochastic density control between Gaussian-mixture distributions under Brownian dynamics by lifting the problem to include trajectory labels, enabling decomposition and simplifying computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The direct Schrödinger bridge for Gaussian mixtures is generally intractable, so the study aims to develop a feasible framework for addressing this computational challenge through a lifted formulation.

Method: The authors propose augmenting trajectories with source-target labels, allowing decomposition into component-specific Schrödinger bridges and reducing the mixture-level assignment to a finite-dimensional entropic coupling problem solvable via Sinkhorn scaling.

Result: The study finds that the projected law obeys the Gaussian-mixture constraints but exhibits a relative entropy gap due to label information loss. Analytical results include the Markov drift for the projected flow, a kinetic-energy bound, and conditions for the gap to vanish.

Conclusion: The proposed lifted framework simplifies the problem by decomposing it, offers new insights into entropic and dynamic properties, and provides numerical demonstrations of density and shape control outcomes.

Abstract: We study stochastic density control between Gaussian-mixture endpoint distributions under Brownian prior dynamics. Since the direct Schrödinger bridge between Gaussian mixtures is generally not available in closed form, we introduce a lifted path-space construction in which each trajectory is augmented with a source--target component label. Consequently, the problem decomposes into Gaussian component-to-component Schrödinger bridges with explicit marginal, drift, and cost formulas, while the mixture-level assignment reduces to a finite-dimensional entropic coupling problem with a Sinkhorn scaling form. We then analyze the projection obtained by discarding or forgetting the label. By construction, the projected law satisfies the original Gaussian-mixture endpoint constraints, but its relative entropy generally differs from the lifted relative entropy by a nonnegative conditional label-information gap. This gap reveals a path-space obstruction: the lifted optimizer cannot, in general, be identified with the direct unlabeled Schrödinger bridge after projection. We also derive the posterior-averaged Markov drift associated with the projected marginal flow, prove a kinetic-energy upper bound, and identify a common path-potential condition under which the projection gap vanishes. Several numerical illustrations showing density and shape control are recorded for a self-contained exposition.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.GN'></div>

# q-fin.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [906] [AI-Driven Alpha Decay: Algorithmic Homogenization, Reflexive Signal Erosion, and the Paradox of Intelligent Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23905)
*Shuchen Meng,Xupeng Chen*

Main category: q-fin.GN

TL;DR: This paper argues that widespread AI adoption in investment strategies leads to diminishing returns due to signal crowding, signal erosion, and competition. It models and validates these effects empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study explores the impact of increasing AI adoption on financial market dynamics, particularly on the longevity of investment strategies' profitability.

Method: The authors derive a mathematical model for the alpha half-life of investment signals, validate it through empirical SEC 13F data, and simulate market events like the Flash Crash to analyze fragility.

Result: The paper shows that increased AI adoption accelerates the decay of investment strategies' returns, shortens their signal lifespan, and increases market convergence among portfolios.

Conclusion: The findings highlight a trade-off between financial market efficiency and systemic fragility, as well as the self-defeating nature of AI-driven investment strategies at scale.

Abstract: We show that AI-driven investment strategies are inherently self-defeating at scale. As AI adoption rises, three mutually reinforcing channels -- signal crowding, performative signal erosion, and Red Queen competition -- compress excess returns. We derive the alpha half-life $h(φ) = \ln 2/[θ+ δ(φ)]$, where $θ$ is the natural mean-reversion rate and $δ(φ) = Nφρa/λ(φ)$ is the AI-accelerated decay component, which is convex-decreasing in adoption. At current adoption levels ($φ\approx 0.7$, $ρ\approx 0.6$), the model implies signal half-lives of 18 months versus 5-7 years pre-AI. We establish four theoretical results. First, the alpha half-life theorem: signal lifespans are convex-decreasing in AI adoption. Second, a signal extinction cascade: beyond a critical threshold $φ^*$, the decay of one signal class triggers accelerated competition for remaining signals. Third, a Red Queen impossibility: in the monoculture equilibrium, net alpha is identically zero despite heavy AI investment. Fourth, a fragility-efficiency tradeoff: the adoption level maximizing price discovery strictly exceeds the level minimizing systemic fragility. Empirical validation calibrates portfolio convergence to SEC Form 13F filing patterns (99.5 million holdings, 2013-2024), documenting that simulated institutional portfolio convergence increases by 42% over the sample period. We examine simulated hedge fund return dynamics showing declining cross-sectional dispersion among AI-adopting funds, and simulate the 2010 Flash Crash to illustrate fragility consequences.

</details>


<div id='math.PR'></div>

# math.PR [[Back]](#toc)

### [907] [Radial Extremality for LRU Caching and the Fill--Holst Conjecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26107)
*Christopher D. Long*

Main category: math.PR

TL;DR: The paper proves that in the context of an LRU cache, the uniform popularity vector globally minimizes the stationary hit rate, confirming improved performance along specific vectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the optimization and properties of the stationary hit rate of LRU caches and extend understanding of cache performance in relation to popularity vectors.

Method: An analysis leveraging the exponential-age representation of LRU caches, deriving a pair-square formula for the radial derivative, and a partial proof of the Fill-Holst conjecture within the context of move-to-front models.

Result: It proves that the LRU hit rate strictly increases along rays from the uniform vector and identifies unique properties of the stationary search-cost distribution tied to cache optimization.

Conclusion: The study confirms the uniform vector’s optimality in minimizing the LRU cache hit rate, providing partial verification of the Fill-Holst conjecture and advancing insights into monotonicity properties.

Abstract: For the independent reference model with popularity vector $p\inΔ_N^\circ$, let $H_C(p)$ denote the exact stationary hit rate of an LRU cache of capacity $C$. We prove that, for every $1\le C<N$, the uniform popularity vector is the unique global minimizer of $H_C$ on the interior simplex. More sharply, along every nonconstant segment from the uniform vector to an interior point, the LRU hit rate is strictly increasing. The proof uses the standard exponential-age representation of the stationary LRU cache and gives an explicit positive pair-square formula for the radial derivative. Equivalently, for the move-to-front rule, the stationary search-cost distribution improves strictly in the usual stochastic order along every nonconstant ray away from uniform. This proves the radial restriction of the Fill--Holst Schur-concavity conjecture for move-to-front search-cost tails. In particular, all LRU miss probabilities and all nonconstant nondecreasing stack-depth costs decrease strictly along such rays. The result is radial rather than Schur-convex: full majorization monotonicity for LRU is known to fail, and the proof identifies the special positivity that survives on rays from the uniform vector.

</details>


### [908] [Exact Variance and Fano Factor for Arbitrary Level Crossings in Stationary Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25278)
*Shivang Rawat,Flaviano Morone,David J. Heeger,Stefano Martiniani*

Main category: math.PR

TL;DR: This paper explores level-crossing statistics in smooth stationary Gaussian processes, extending beyond the traditional mean-rate analysis by deriving exact formulas for variance and Fano factors to reveal crossing behavior patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of mean rate analysis (like Kac-Rice formula) in level crossings, which ignores the temporal correlation structure of crossings that is essential for understanding clustering or dispersal over time.

Method: The approach involves deriving exact analytical formulae for the variance and Fano factors of arbitrary level crossings in smooth stationary Gaussian processes, in order to examine the influence of temporal correlations.

Result: The analysis reveals that the temporal correlation structure can result in sub-Poissonian or super-Poissonian crossing statistics depending on system properties like oscillatory behavior, damping, or relaxation dynamics.

Conclusion: This work provides deeper insights into temporal organization of level crossings and extends traditional tools, enabling better parameter estimation and model selection in applications involving Gaussian processes.

Abstract: Understanding the statistics of level crossings in stochastic processes is crucial across many scientific disciplines. The traditional Kac-Rice formula gives the mean rate of level crossings and has found broad use. However, that mean rate captures only a coarse summary of the crossing process. It depends entirely on local properties of the stochastic process at a given instant and is therefore blind to the correlation structure of the process over time. To understand whether crossing events, such as neuronal spikes, tend to cluster in time, spread apart, or exhibit more complex temporal organization, one must go beyond the mean rate and study higher-order crossing statistics. Here we go beyond the mean by deriving the exact analytical formulae for the variance and Fano factor of arbitrary level crossings in smooth stationary Gaussian processes. Our exact solution reveals how the full temporal correlation structure dictates whether crossings cluster or become regular. In systems with oscillatory correlations, such as a stochastic damped harmonic oscillator, a recent crossing suppresses an immediate subsequent one, producing sub-Poissonian statistics. However, as damping increases and oscillations disappear, a large and slow excursion above the threshold can produce multiple closely spaced crossings, yielding super-Poissonian statistics. In purely relaxational, non-oscillatory systems, such as a mean-reverting process driven by Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise, the competition between the timescales of the driving noise and system relaxation produces a richer landscape, including reentrant transitions between sub- and super-Poissonian statistics as the threshold level is varied. Taken together, the exact variance and Fano factor derived here complement the Kac-Rice mean rate, enabling more robust parameter estimation and model selection across any setting where Gaussian processes are used.

</details>


### [909] [Error estimates for tamed Euler and Randomized Euler schemes for SDEs with locally Lipschitz drift with applications to non-logconcave sampling and optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24937)
*Iosif Lytras,Angelos Ntousis*

Main category: math.PR

TL;DR: The paper focuses on improving numerical methods for unstable stochastic processes and achieving effective sampling from certain non-log-concave distributions. Two new schemes, kTULA and tRLMC, are analyzed, with strong theoretical guarantees in error estimation and computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling from non-log-concave distributions under locally Lipschitz and super-linearly growing drift is unstable using classical methods, necessitating the development of alternative approaches with better error guarantees.

Method: The authors analyze the KL-accelerated tamed unadjusted Langevin algorithm (kTULA) and introduce a new randomized midpoint scheme called tRLMC. They also establish two novel local-error frameworks for error estimation.

Result: The paper proves near-optimal iteration complexity of $\widetilde{O}(\varepsilon^{-1/2})$ for kTULA and $\widetilde{O}(\varepsilon^{-1})$ for tRLMC, providing finite-time guarantees in KL divergence, total variation, and Wasserstein distance.

Conclusion: Both schemes, kTULA and tRLMC, provide robust error bounds and computational complexities, making them effective for non-log-concave sampling and non-convex optimization problems.

Abstract: In this paper, we study the numerical discretization of stochastic differential equations with locally Lipschitz, super-linearly growing drift, and the resulting implications for sampling from non-log-concave distributions satisfying a logarithmic Sobolev inequality. In this regime, the classical Euler--Maruyama scheme underlying the unadjusted Langevin algorithm (ULA) is known to be unstable. We analyze the KL-accelerated tamed unadjusted Langevin algorithm (kTULA) and introduce a new tamed randomized midpoint scheme, termed tRLMC. Building on the shifted-composition approach of \cite{chewi2024local}, we develop two new local-error frameworks that yield finite-time, non-asymptotic error estimates against the underlying SDE -- in KL divergence for kTULA, and in total variation for tRLMC -- valid for general locally Lipschitz drift. Specializing these frameworks to the sampling problem under a logarithmic Sobolev inequality, we obtain a near-optimal $\widetilde{O}(\varepsilon^{-1/2})$ iteration complexity for kTULA in KL divergence, with corresponding guarantees in total variation and Wasserstein distance. We further establish, for the first time, a non-asymptotic guarantee in total variation for a tamed randomized Langevin scheme under super-linear drift growth, together with the corresponding Wasserstein-distance bound, both with $\widetilde{O}(\varepsilon^{-1})$ complexity for tRLMC. As a consequence, both schemes yield non-asymptotic bounds for a non-convex excess-risk optimization problem.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [910] [Multimodal Alignment and Preference Optimization for Zero-Shot Conditional RNA Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23961)
*Roman Klypa,Alberto Bietti,Sergei Grudinin*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: This paper proposes Moirain, a multi-stage model framework for generating RNA sequences optimized for protein interactions, demonstrating improved results compared to current approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently designing RNA sequences that interact with specific proteins remains an unsolved challenge in computational biology, despite advancements in machine learning and protein design techniques.

Method: The method involves pretraining on diverse RNA datasets, followed by multimodal supervised fine-tuning to condition RNA synthesis on protein features, and applying Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to enhance functional fitness while preserving sequence authenticity.

Result: The Moirain models (Base, Multi, and DPO) deliver RNA sequences that are novel, diverse, biologically plausible, and exhibit better binding affinities than existing methods.

Conclusion: Moirain establishes a robust framework for conditional RNA sequence generation, addressing a gap in successful protein interaction design and advancing the applicability of RNA-protein research.

Abstract: The design of RNA molecules that interact with specific proteins is a critical challenge in experimental and computational biology. Despite recent progress in natural language modeling and deep learning-based protein design, there remains significant room to improve the frequency of successful interactions and the authenticity of generated sequences for functional applications. In this work, we frame conditional RNA sequence generation as a multi-stage alignment problem, introducing Moirain: a suite of models optimized via multimodal supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Our approach begins with large-scale pretraining on diverse RNA corpora to capture the fundamental grammars of sequence plausibility. To achieve target-specific generation, we employ a multimodal SFT architecture that conditions RNA synthesis on protein structural and sequential features. Finally, we leverage DPO to refine the model using synthetic interaction data: taking advantage of DPO's unique ability to navigate non-aligned preference spaces, we improve functional fitness without collapsing the learned natural distribution. Extensive evaluation of the Moirain series (Moirain-Base, -Multi, and -DPO) demonstrates that our framework consistently produces novel, diverse, and biologically plausible RNA sequences with superior binding affinities compared to existing baselines.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [911] [Posture Clip: Sit properly or I wont let you work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25664)
*Arka Majhi,Aparajita Mondal*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: PostureClip is a collar-clipped device designed to improve posture by providing feedback and interventions, resulting in significant posture improvements during sedentary work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address detrimental effects of poor sitting posture on health and productivity during prolonged laptop/PC use.

Method: A controlled 4-week experiment with 165 participants assigned to intervention groups (PostureClip with/without feedback) or a control group. Measurements of posture angle, bent angle duration, and user feedback were analyzed.

Result: The intervention group using PostureClip with feedback showed statistically significant improvements in posture angle and reduced bent angle duration compared to other groups.

Conclusion: PostureClip, particularly when integrated with user feedback mechanisms, is effective in improving posture awareness and habits during prolonged sedentary work.

Abstract: Poor posture is a significant concern due to its detrimental effects on health and productivity. This paper presents a collar-clipped device called PostureClip, designed to restrict users from sitting and working at a bent angle, by blacking out the screen and resuming on correcting posture, thereby promoting better posture. The device integrates sensors and feedback mechanisms to provide real-time posture feedback to users.
  To evaluate the effectiveness of PostureClip, a controlled experiment was conducted with participants (n=165) who were working on a laptop/PC for over 6 hours per day. The participants were randomly assigned to both the intervention group (IG1,n=54 ; IG2,n=55), which used the collar-clipped device, and the control group (CG, n=56), which did not use the device. IG1 didn't get feedback while IG2 got feedback from the device by notifying and further darkening the screen. The study was conducted in the office environment of the participants, for 4 weeks, and metrics such as posture angle, duration of bent angle, and user feedback were collected.
  Analysis revealed significant improvements in posture angle (p<0.001) and significant reduction in bent angle duration (p<0.01) for participants' group using PostureClip with feedback and compared to the group without feedback and the control group (who were not intervened). The qualitative analysis of user feedback highlighted the device's ease of use, effectiveness in providing timely feedback, and positive impact on participants' awareness and habits regarding posture. These results indicate that PostureClip is an effective tool for promoting better posture during sedentary work.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [912] [Rubato: Transcribing Piano Music with Timestamps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24291)
*Nazif Can Tamer,Victoria Ebert,Guang Yang,Noah A. Smith*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new model, "Rubato," for transcribing audio into timestamped sheet music with high accuracy, leveraging a novel textual representation "InterMo" for training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for advanced transcription systems that can convert musical recordings into usable, timestamped sheet music for applications like practice, performance analysis, and musicological research.

Method: Developed an encoder-decoder model, "Rubato," trained with textual representation "InterMo". It uses prompt-based training to handle multiple related tasks like note placement and beat detection.

Result: Rubato demonstrated superior performance in creating timestamped piano sheet music from audio compared to cascade-based models. It even outperformed cascade models that were provided ground-truth MIDI, emphasizing the advantages of its new representational structure.

Conclusion: Rubato advances the state-of-the-art in audio-to-score transcription by introducing better representations and multi-task capabilities. It sets a new benchmark in accuracy and general versatility for music transcription systems.

Abstract: We consider the conversion of musical recordings into human-readable sheet music annotated with timestamps. Such output lets a listener clearly visualize rubato (temporally expressive playing), a learner diagnose ensemble precision and timing choices against the written music, and a musicology scholar compare performance styles across recordings of the same work. We introduce (1) a prompt-conditioned encoder-decoder model, named Rubato, trained to output (2) a new textual representation for polyphonic music, named InterMo, which we designed for compatibility with sequence-to-sequence training. Our experiments demonstrate that Rubato produces timestamped piano sheet music from audio with higher notational accuracy than the best existing approaches, which are based on cascades. We find that even if the cascade is given ground-truth MIDI instead of audio, Rubato performs better, suggesting that the ceiling of existing approaches is primarily representational, not acoustic. Further, because Rubato is trained on several related tasks (with prompts), it competes with or outperforms the best single-task systems on related but simpler tasks like MIDI note grounding and beat/downbeat detection. A demo is available at https://nctamer.github.io/rubato-transcription .

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [913] [A Reinforcement Learning Inspired Latent Yield Based Adaptive Algorithm Switching Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24436)
*Jayprakash S. Nair,Jimson Mathew,Shivashankar B. Nair*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new method for adaptive algorithm selection using a latent yield mechanism and island models, tested on sorting and robotic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of choosing the most appropriate algorithm in dynamic environments, avoiding suboptimal and unstable switching based solely on momentary performance.

Method: The paper introduces a method inspired by Reinforcement Learning, where rewards and penalties are encapsulated into a latent yield mechanism. It uses island models, which stem from Genetic Algorithms, to encourage parallel exploration and knowledge sharing.

Result: Experimental results on sorting and robotic obstacle avoidance demonstrate the technique's ability to adaptively select algorithms and improve performance.

Conclusion: The study concludes that the proposed approach is computationally efficient and suitable for adaptive algorithm selection in evolving problem spaces.

Abstract: Selecting the most suitable algorithm for a given problem instance remains a challenging task, particularly in online or dynamic environments where problem characteristics evolve over time. Relying solely on instantaneous performance metrics can result in a reactive and unstable behaviour, often leading to suboptimal algorithm switching. This paper introduces a computationally efficient approach for aggregating an algorithm's performance across multiple problem instances that is fairly immune to erratic variations in instance features. Inspired by features inherent to Reinforcement Learning (RL), this technique encapsulates rewards and penalties into a latent yield that, in turn, triggers exploitation and exploration, consequently resulting in adaptive algorithm switching. The proposed technique employs island models, inspired by Genetic Algorithms, to facilitate parallel exploration and performance exchanges among algorithm populations inhabiting local repertoires. Experimental evaluations on sorting algorithms and robotic obstacle avoidance tasks demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the approach, highlighting its potential in domains where adaptive algorithm selection is critical.

</details>


### [914] [SODE: Analyzing Social Dynamics in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23949)
*Inseo Jung,Yoonseok Oh,Kyungryul Back,Jinkyu Kim,Jungbeom Lee*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SODE is a framework introduced to evaluate social dynamics of LLM agents in terms of reciprocity and cooperation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how LLM agents behave within human social interactions to promote sustainable cooperation has been underexplored.

Method: The SODE framework evaluates LLM agents across Direct Reciprocity, Indirect Reciprocity, and Group Dynamics dimensions.

Result: Instruction-tuned models often show vulnerability to exploitation, while reasoning models destabilize cooperation; long-horizon framing improves reciprocal capabilities.

Conclusion: SODE provides a systematic benchmark for aligning AI more effectively with human social dynamics by examining behavioral strategies.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into interactive agents, understanding their behavioral alignment within human social dynamics becomes essential. While behavioral game theory offers a framework to study these interactions, previous work has predominantly relied on outcome-based metrics such as average scores. This focus overlooks the mechanisms that facilitate sustainable cooperation, as identical scores can be derived from vastly different strategies. To bridge this gap, we introduce SODE (Social Dynamics Evaluation), a framework that evaluates LLM agents across three evolutionary dimensions: Direct Reciprocity for strategy adaptation, Indirect Reciprocity for reputation sensitivity, and Group Dynamics for cooperative resilience. Applying SODE reveals systematic divergences: instruction-tuned models often exhibit "passive compliance" that renders them vulnerable to exploitation, while reasoning models prioritize short-horizon optimization, destabilizing long-term cooperation. Notably, we demonstrate that a "long-horizon framing" can unlock reciprocal capabilities in reasoning models. Thus, SODE offers a systematic, mechanism-grounded benchmark for aligning AI agents with complex human social dynamics.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [915] [Catching The Correct Answer Trap: Characterising AI Tutor Blind Spots When Analysing Student Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23925)
*Moiz Imran,Sahan Bulathwela*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper investigates the limitations of language models in identifying flawed reasoning despite reaching correct answers in intelligent tutoring systems, using real math response data from Eedi.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the problem of intelligent tutoring systems failing to detect flawed student reasoning when it leads to correct answers, which compromises the quality of automated feedback.

Method: The study analyzes real student responses to two specific question types in the Eedi mathematics platform and compares the performance of a fine-tuned T5 model and a large language model in detecting flawed reasoning.

Result: The authors found that 71% of failures were concentrated in two types of questions. Improved models had detection accuracies of 84% (T5) and 57% (large model), but false alarms were significant, making current automated methods impractical.

Conclusion: High accuracy alone is insufficient in assessing student reasoning, as significant flaws remain. Combining AI capabilities with human judgment is needed for effective feedback quality.

Abstract: Intelligent tutoring systems increasingly provide automated feedback on student work, but robust feedback requires assessing reasoning, not only final answers. We study a failure mode we call the correct answer trap (CAT): models under-detect misconceptions when students reach a correct answer via flawed reasoning. Analysing real student responses from the Eedi mathematics platform, we show that 71% of these failures concentrate in just two question types, both sharing a common structure where flawed reasoning happens to produce the correct numerical answer. Comparing a fine-tuned T5 with a frontier large language model, we find that improved capabilities reduce but do not eliminate the problem (84% vs 57% detection accuracy). Even the best-performing model generates roughly four false alarms for every genuine detection, making stand-alone screening impractical at realistic class sizes. Our findings demonstrate that high overall accuracy can mask critical failures in reasoning assessment, and that careful analysis of student reasoning still benefits from human judgment.

</details>


### [916] [Artificial Effort](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23920)
*Federico Belotti,Stefano Coniglio,Antonio Cosma,Francesco Fallucchi*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how advancements in AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) challenge the validity of real-effort tasks in experimental economics, showing that many such tasks can now be automated cheaply and precisely.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to assess whether real-effort tasks, traditionally reliant on human performance, maintain their validity amidst the rise of powerful AI systems and LLMs.

Method: Researchers tested 8 canonical real-effort tasks across 23 LLMs from three major providers to evaluate their ability to solve these tasks accurately and cost-effectively. The impact of verbal monetary incentives on LLM performance was also examined.

Result: The study found that most real-effort tasks can be automated with high accuracy and low cost using LLMs. Only a limited number of tasks resist automation, and the capabilities of midtier LLMs are quickly catching up with top-tier models.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that real-effort tasks are increasingly susceptible to automation through LLMs, challenging their validity in unsupervised experimental settings, especially when participants could outsource these tasks.

Abstract: Real-effort tasks, in which participants perform cognitively costly activities whose outcomes depend on actual performance, are widely used in experimental economics. Their validity, however, rests on the assumption that a human performs them. We study whether this assumption still holds in the era of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLMs). Using 8 canonical real-effort tasks and 23 LLMs from three major providers, we show that most tasks can now be solved accurately and at a negligible cost, while only a few resist automation. Performance improves with each model generation, and midtier models are rapidly closing the gap with frontier ones, broadening the set of widely accessible models that can automate these tasks. Additionally, we show that verbally offering monetary incentives has no effect on LLM performance. Our findings establish a boundary condition for the use of real-effort tasks in unsupervised settings: when participants can cheaply outsource task completion to an LLM, observed performance may no longer reflect genuine human effort.

</details>


### [917] [Authority Signals in Claude AI Health Citations: A Descriptive Analysis Using the Authority Signals Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23921)
*Erin T. Jacques,Erela Datuowei,Elizabeth Quaye,Corey H. Basch,Arijit Chatterjee,Juanita Davis*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the credibility and authority of sources cited by Anthropic's Claude AI in consumer health responses using the Authority Signals Framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the reliability and authority of sources cited by Claude AI in health-related contexts, especially as the LLM is positioned for healthcare applications.

Method: The study analyzed 10,038 citations from HealthSearchQA (a dataset of 3,172 health questions), applying the Authority Signals Framework to a focused sample of 542 sources.

Result: 97.8% of citations came from established institutional sources. The most-cited types were Medical Institutions, Government Resources, and Professional Associations. Mayo Clinic was the dominant single source (24.7%).

Conclusion: Claude AI predominantly cites credible institutions, but its citation criteria vary. The research establishes a benchmark for evaluating AI-driven health information, emphasizing the Authority Signals Framework's utility for cross-platform assessments.

Abstract: This study seeks to determine the authority signals used by Anthropic's Claude AI in its presentation of sources when answering consumer health questions. While there exists a great deal of discourse around the quality of health citations that LLMs produce, there is limited information on the integrity of the sources the citations originate from, and to what extent the sources are, from what health professionals would consider, credible sources. This descriptive cross-sectional study used data from HealthSearchQA, which contains 3,172 consumer health questions curated by Google Research. After exclusions, a final dataset of 3,075 questions yielding 10,038 citations was analyzed. The Authority Signals Framework (Jacques et al., 2026) was applied to examine 10 authority signals across four domains for a disproportionate stratified sample of 542 sources. Established institutional sources accounted for 97.8% of all citations (n = 9,818). Medical Institutions were the most frequently cited organization type (36.5%), followed by Government Resources (31.6%) and Professional Associations (28.4%). Commercial Health Information comprised 2.2% (n = 220). The top 10 organizations accounted for 57.8% of all citations, with Mayo Clinic alone representing 24.7%. Among commercial sources in the focused sample, 86.4% displayed medical review statements, 82.5% used schema markup, and 71.8% had comprehensive content, while traditional institutional sources appeared in Claude's citations with or without these same markers. As Anthropic positions Claude for HIPAA-ready healthcare applications, these findings establish a baseline for Claude's citation behavior and demonstrate the utility of the Authority Signals Framework as a tool for ongoing, cross-platform evaluation of AI-mediated health information.

</details>


### [918] [High-Risk AI Systems and the Problem of Identity in the European AI Act](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23922)
*Andrea Ferrario*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper critiques the EU Artificial Intelligence Act (AIA) for lacking clear criteria for determining AI system identity over time and proposes the function+ framework to clarify these issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inadequacy of the EU AIA in providing a clear criterion for AI system identity, especially for regulatory compliance and lifecycle governance.

Method: The paper uses the function+ framework that focuses on intended function and context-sensitive trustworthiness to define AI system identity and create a decision-making flow for governance settings.

Result: The function+ framework correlates AIA lifecycle obligations with identity components and operationalizes system identity through auditable methods, making it applicable in crucial areas like procurement and liability.

Conclusion: The authors recommend (1) more precise reporting of AI systems' intended purpose, and (2) audited standards for reporting trustworthiness to aid regulatory effectiveness and comparability.

Abstract: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (AIA) establishes a lifecycle governance regime for high-risk AI systems built around ex-ante conformity assessment, post-market monitoring, and re-assessment upon "substantial modification." These obligations presuppose AI identity judgments: regulators and providers must decide when an updated system remains the same system over time. In this work, we show how this logic is clarified by the function+ framework of artifact identity, which individuates AI systems by their intended function together with context-sensitive criteria of appropriate functioning, captured as "AI trustworthiness." We further argue that the AIA does not provide an internal, auditable criterion for synchronic identity--when two AI systems at a given time should count as the same for regulatory purposes--and instead largely defers such sameness determinations to sectoral or harmonization instruments. function+ supplies a synchronic identity test anchored in intended function and trustworthiness profiles and levels, making synchronic identity decisions inspectable in governance settings such as procurement, liability, and market surveillance. Our contribution is a conceptual and auditing lens: we provide a correspondence map between AIA lifecycle obligations and function+ identity components, and we make the synchronic case operationally legible via a minimal decision flow for audit and dispute contexts. We conclude with two implementation-facing recommendations: (1) more precise, testable reporting of intended purpose, and (2) standardized, auditable trustworthiness reporting that supports comparability over time and across deployments.

</details>


### [919] [KT4EQG: Personalized Exercise Question Generation via Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23933)
*Xinyi Gao,Qiucheng Wu,Lu Ding,Q. Vera Liao,Kaizhi Qian,Ying Xu,Shiyu Chang,Yang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper introduces KT4EQG, a personalized Educational Question Generation framework leveraging knowledge tracing to create customized questions that improve students' learning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of fine-grained personalization in current EQG systems and enhance student learning by tailoring questions to individual knowledge states.

Method: The framework, KT4EQG, uses a knowledge tracing (KT) model to assess a student's knowledge state and identify suitable knowledge concepts for practice. It then trains an LLM-based generator to create questions grounded in those concepts.

Result: Experiments on the XES3G5M and MOOCRadar datasets demonstrate that KT4EQG generates more effective, personalized questions compared to methods with limited or no personalization.

Conclusion: The proposed KT4EQG framework successfully integrates KT models and question generation techniques, highlighting the potential for personalized EQG to improve educational outcomes.

Abstract: Educational Question Generation (EQG) aims to synthesize customized exercise questions that enhance student learning. An effective EQG system should ideally personalize questions for each student by modeling the student's knowledge state and generating questions that provide the greatest learning benefit. However, few existing EQG approaches are able to achieve such fine-grained personalization. In this paper, we explore how EQG can benefit from knowledge tracing (KT), which models students' knowledge states based on historical performance and predicts future performance. We propose KT4EQG, a personalized EQG framework that generates effective questions for individual students under the guidance of a KT model. Specifically, KT4EQG seeks to maximize a student's potential improvement in overall knowledge mastery by leveraging the KT model to select the most suitable knowledge concept for the student to practice. An LLM-based question generator is then trained to produce a question faithfully grounded in the selected concept. Experimental results on XES3G5M and MOOCRadar show that KT4EQG consistently generates more effective questions than methods with limited or no personalization.

</details>


### [920] [AI-Driven Controlled Environment Agriculture as Resilient Infrastructure for U.S. Fresh-Produce Supply Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23946)
